choices: To step voltage up or down
To protect wiring by interrupting overcurrent
To store electrical energy for later use
To filter noise from the alternator output
multipleChoice.explanation: Fuses and breakers provide overcurrent protection, primarily for the wiring and, by extension, the airframe and nearby components. A fuse is a one-time sacrificial element; a circuit breaker can be reset after a trip.

practice.question: A student asks why a 10 A circuit breaker is installed in a circuit when the load itself only draws 3 A normally. What is the correct explanation?
choices: The breaker is oversized and should match the load draw exactly
The breaker protects the WIRING from excessive current during faults; it is sized to the wire's safe ampacity, not the load's nominal draw
The breaker will not trip at 3 A because the load is below its rating
The breaker should be sized smaller than the load for safety reasons
practice.explanation: Circuit breakers are sized to protect wire ampacity with some margin, not to match exact load. A 3 A load on 14 AWG wire (rated roughly 15 A) might be protected by a 10 A breaker — the breaker protects the wire from short-circuit overcurrent while allowing normal load current to pass. Sizing the breaker exactly to the load would cause nuisance tripping on startup surges.

preassessment.question: What is the primary purpose of a fuse or circuit breaker?
choices: To regulate voltage
To protect wiring from overcurrent
To store energy
To convert AC to DC

questions.learningObjective: Identify the farad as the SI unit of capacitance
questions.fullObjective: Recognize capacitance as the ability of a device to store electrical charge, measured in farads. Understand that 1 farad is a very large unit and that practical capacitors are rated in microfarads, nanofarads, or picofarads.

flashcard.definition: The farad is the SI unit of capacitance. One farad equals one coulomb of charge stored per volt applied. Practical capacitor values are expressed in microfarads (µF, 10⁻⁶ F), nanofarads (nF, 10⁻⁹ F), or picofarads (pF, 10⁻¹² F).
flashcard.analogy: A farad represents how many 'gallons of charge' a capacitor can store per volt of pressure applied. One full farad is enormous — the size of a large industrial bucket.

jeopardy.question: Capacitance is measured in this SI unit.
choices: Henries
Ohms
Farads
Hertz
jeopardy.explanation: Capacitance is measured in farads. Inductance is measured in henries. Resistance is measured in ohms. Frequency is measured in hertz.

multipleChoice.question: Which of the following is the SI unit of electrical capacitance?
choices: Henry
Ohm
Farad
Volt
multipleChoice.explanation: The farad (F) is the SI unit of capacitance. One farad is a very large value; practical capacitors are usually expressed in microfarads (µF), nanofarads (nF), or picofarads (pF). The henry is for inductance, the ohm is for resistance, and the volt is for potential difference.

practice.question: A schematic shows a capacitor labeled '10 µF.' What does the '10 µF' notation indicate about the component?
choices: Its capacitance is 10 millifarads (10 × 10⁻³ F)
Its capacitance is 10 megafarads (10 × 10⁶ F)
Its capacitance is 10 microfarads (10 × 10⁻⁶ F)
Its operating voltage is 10 volts; capacitance is unspecified
practice.explanation: The prefix 'µ' (mu) denotes 'micro,' meaning one millionth. A 10 µF capacitor has a capacitance of 10 × 10⁻⁶ farads. Other common prefixes include milli (m, 10⁻³), nano (n, 10⁻⁹), and pico (p, 10⁻¹²). Capacitor values on schematics always specify capacitance, not voltage — voltage ratings are listed separately.

preassessment.question: What is the SI unit of electrical capacitance?
choices: Henry
Ohm
Farad
Volt

questions.learningObjective: Define an aircraft electrical bus
questions.fullObjective: Describe an aircraft electrical bus as a common conductor (typically a bar or heavy wire) to which multiple loads connect in parallel, enabling centralized power distribution and protection.

flashcard.definition: An electrical bus is a common conductor that multiple loads share. Loads connect in parallel off the bus, each typically through its own circuit breaker. Aircraft commonly have multiple busses (main, essential, avionics, battery) with switchable cross-feeds between them.
flashcard.analogy: An electrical bus is analogous to a shared water main: every household tap branches off the same pipe.

jeopardy.question: An aircraft electrical bus is best described as this.
choices: A single large battery
The engine-driven alternator
A protection panel containing only fuses
A common conductor that multiple loads share
jeopardy.explanation: A bus is a shared conductor (literally a 'bar' in the term 'busbar') from which loads branch in parallel. This architecture centralizes power distribution, simplifies wiring, and isolates faults through dedicated circuit breakers on each branch.

multipleChoice.question: Which description best matches an aircraft electrical bus?
choices: A single large battery
The alternator's armature winding
Only the protection panel
A common conductor from which multiple
multipleChoice.explanation: An electrical bus is a shared distribution conductor. Loads connect in parallel off the bus, each typically through its own circuit breaker. Aircraft often have multiple busses — main, essential, avionics, battery — with switchable cross-feeds between them.

practice.question: An aircraft has a 'main bus' and a separate 'essential bus.' What does the essential-bus concept allow the pilot to do during an electrical emergency?
choices: Disconnect the alternator to save battery capacity
Recharge the battery in flight
Route power around a failed alternator
Isolate the essential bus and shed all non-essential loads so that critical avionics and instruments continue to receive battery power for as long as possible
practice.explanation: The essential-bus architecture isolates critical loads (communication, navigation, pitot heat, critical instruments) so they can continue operating on battery power after load shedding. This is specifically designed for post-alternator-failure endurance, which is why emergency checklists include 'shed non-essential loads' as an early step.

preassessment.question: What is an aircraft electrical bus?
choices: A battery
An alternator winding
A fuse panel only
A common shared conductor

questions.learningObjective: Define parasitic current draw and identify common causes
questions.fullObjective: Describe parasitic current draw as unintended battery drain when the master switch is OFF, and identify common causes including stuck relays, leaky electronic modules, and miswired connections to the always-hot side of the battery.

flashcard.definition: Parasitic current draw is unintended battery drain that occurs when the master switch is OFF. Common causes include stuck relays, leaky electronic modules, and wires mistakenly landed on the always-hot side of the battery contactor. The result is a slowly dying battery over days or weeks.
flashcard.analogy: A parasitic draw is like a slow tire leak: it is difficult to detect, but it will flatten the battery overnight or over a weekend.

jeopardy.question: This is the best description of a parasitic current draw in an aircraft electrical system.
choices: Normal current draw during engine start
Current flowing from the alternator while the engine is running
Unintended current drain with the master switch OFF
Current drawn by the navigation lights at night
jeopardy.explanation: 'Parasitic' refers to current that should not be flowing. It is usually a clue to a stuck relay, a wiring mistake, or a leaky electronic module drawing from always-hot battery wiring.

multipleChoice.question: How is a parasitic current draw BEST described in an aircraft electrical system?
choices: Normal current during engine start
Alternator output current during cruise
Unintended current drain with the master switch OFF
Current required to recharge the battery
multipleChoice.explanation: A parasitic draw is an unintended drain. The standard shop test is to switch everything off, place an ammeter in series with the battery's negative cable, and read the residual current. Anything above a few milliamps indicates a problem.

practice.question: A pilot complains that the battery is always dead after the aircraft sits for a week. You suspect a parasitic draw. Which is the MOST common cause in general aviation aircraft?
choices: The battery naturally self-discharges fully every week
Static electricity buildup on the airframe
A stuck relay, a leaky avionics module, or a wire inadvertently landed on the 'always-hot' side of the battery contactor
The battery cap is vented, allowing charge to escape
practice.explanation: Common culprits include a relay coil that failed to drop out after master-off, an avionics unit whose 'off' state still draws several milliamps, or — most often — a wire landed on the battery side of the master contactor instead of on the bus side, which bypasses the master switch entirely. A parasitic draw hunt typically starts at the battery with an ammeter in series and the master switch OFF.

preassessment.question: What is parasitic current draw in an aircraft?
choices: Normal engine-start current
Alternator charging current
Unintended drain with the master switch OFF
Battery charging current

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of the DC electrical system in a typical GA aircraft
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the DC electrical system starts the engine, powers avionics, instruments, and lighting during flight, and recharges the battery while the engine is running.

flashcard.term: Purpose of the DC electrical system
flashcard.definition: The aircraft DC electrical system performs three primary functions: it supplies cranking current to start the engine, powers all DC avionics, instruments, and lighting during flight, and uses the alternator or generator to recharge the battery while the engine is running.
flashcard.analogy: The DC electrical system combines a starter, utility power source, and trickle charger in a single aircraft system.

jeopardy.question: This is the PRIMARY purpose of the DC electrical system in a typical general aviation aircraft.
choices: To produce high-voltage AC for large motors
To provide hydraulic pressure for flight controls
To start the engine and power avionics, instruments, and lighting during flight
To serve as a backup only, while AC performs most of the work
jeopardy.explanation: Starting, avionics, instruments, and lighting are the day-to-day jobs of the DC system. Most GA aircraft do not have an AC system. Larger aircraft add AC for certain motors and instruments, but DC still handles engine starting and critical avionics in both classes.

multipleChoice.question: What is the PRIMARY purpose of the DC electrical system in a typical general aviation aircraft?
choices: To generate high-voltage AC for large motors
To provide hydraulic pressure for flight controls
To start the engine and power DC avionics
To serve only as emergency backup power
multipleChoice.explanation: The DC system performs the primary electrical functions in a GA aircraft: engine starting plus continuous in-flight power for radios, instruments, and lights. During flight, the alternator both supplies current to active loads and recharges the battery at the same time.

practice.question: A new aircraft owner asks, 'What does the aircraft's DC electrical system actually do while I'm flying?' Which response is the best one-sentence answer?
choices: It only powers the radio — everything else is mechanical
It provides the main thrust by driving an electric motor
It starts the engine, and in flight it powers all avionics and instruments while the alternator simultaneously recharges the battery
It operates only during emergencies
practice.explanation: The DC system has two primary roles: engine starting (a one-time event at each flight) and continuous in-flight power distribution to avionics, instruments, and lighting, while the alternator simultaneously supplies loads and recharges the battery.

preassessment.question: What is the primary role of the DC electrical system in a typical GA aircraft?
choices: Producing high-voltage AC
Providing hydraulic pressure
Starting the engine and powering
Serving only as a backup

questions.learningObjective: Identify the Label field in an ARINC 429 data word
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that every 32-bit ARINC 429 data word begins with an 8-bit Label field that encodes the parameter type (altitude, airspeed, heading, etc.) in octal, allowing receivers to filter inbound traffic by parameter.

flashcard.definition: The first 8 bits of every 32-bit ARINC 429 word. The Label is an octal-coded identifier of WHAT parameter the word contains — for example, label 203 (octal) = barometric altitude, label 206 = computed airspeed. Receivers use the Label to filter the transmitter's stream and pluck out only the parameters they care about.
flashcard.analogy: Think of the Label as the subject line of an email. The transmitter broadcasts every parameter it knows about. Each receiver reads only the subject lines that matter to it and ignores the rest.

jeopardy.question: This 8-bit field at the start of every ARINC 429 word tells a receiver which parameter — altitude, airspeed, heading, etc. — the word contains.
choices: Label
Preamble
Destination tag
Parity block
jeopardy.explanation: The Label is the first 8 bits of every 32-bit ARINC 429 word. It is octal-coded (for example, label 203 = altitude, 206 = computed airspeed). Receivers filter the transmitter's stream by Label and accept only the parameters they need. ARINC 429 does not use a separate preamble or destination tag, and parity is a single bit at the end of the word, not a block.

multipleChoice.question: Every ARINC 429 data word begins with an 8-bit field that identifies what parameter the word represents (altitude, airspeed, heading, etc.). What is the name of that field?
choices: Label
Preamble header
Destination tag
Parity block
multipleChoice.explanation: ARINC 429 words are 32 bits long; the first 8 bits are the Label, octal-coded to identify the parameter (e.g., 203 = altitude, 206 = computed airspeed). Receivers filter incoming words by Label and ignore anything they do not need. ARINC 429 does not use a separate preamble or destination tag, and parity is a single trailing bit.

practice.question: An MFD is wired to receive ARINC 429 data from an air data computer that broadcasts 18 different parameters on the same pair. The MFD only needs altitude and airspeed. How does the MFD select just the two parameters it wants?
choices: It reads the 8-bit Label at the start of each word and accepts only the Labels it cares about
The air data computer sends only altitude and airspeed to that specific MFD over a dedicated wire
It uses the destination address field in the word to match its own address
It requests specific parameters from the air data computer using a reverse-channel command
practice.explanation: ARINC 429 is one-way broadcast — the transmitter continuously sends every parameter it has, and each receiver filters the stream by Label. There is no destination addressing and no reverse channel from the MFD back to the air data computer. The MFD simply reads the 8-bit Label at the start of each word and keeps the ones it wants.

preassessment.question: What is the first 8 bits of an ARINC 429 word called?
choices: Label
Preamble
Destination tag
Sync word

questions.learningObjective: Explain the purpose of the MIL-STD-1553 dual-redundant bus architecture
questions.fullObjective: Understand that MIL-STD-1553 runs two parallel cables (Bus A and Bus B) so traffic can continue if one cable is physically damaged or electrically faulted, providing single-fault tolerance for flight-critical applications.

flashcard.definition: MIL-STD-1553 runs TWO physically separate cables (labeled Bus A and Bus B) that carry the same traffic. The Bus Controller normally uses Bus A; if it detects errors, it retries the same command on Bus B. Both cables run in parallel through the aircraft, typically in different bundles, so a single wire bundle getting damaged does not take down the whole system.
flashcard.analogy: It is like having two identical phone lines to the same house. If one line is cut, calls still get through on the other. For flight-critical systems, this redundancy is what makes 1553 acceptable where ARINC 429 and RS-232 are not.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary reason MIL-STD-1553 wires every Remote Terminal to two parallel cables (Bus A and Bus B).
choices: To double the data rate for high-bandwidth applications
Redundancy — traffic continues on the other bus if one fails
To separate command traffic from response traffic
To let two Bus Controllers share the bus workload
jeopardy.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 was designed for flight-critical military applications where a single wire fault must not disable the system. The two buses carry identical traffic; the Bus Controller normally uses Bus A, and if it sees errors it retries on Bus B. The data rate is 1 Mbps on either bus — using both does NOT double it. And 1553 allows only one active Bus Controller at a time.

multipleChoice.question: A MIL-STD-1553 Remote Terminal is wired to two separate bus cables, commonly labeled A and B. What is the primary reason for this dual-bus architecture?
choices: To double the data rate for high-bandwidth parameters
To provide redundancy so traffic continues if one bus fails
To separate command traffic from response traffic on different wires
To allow two Bus Controllers to share the workload
multipleChoice.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 is dual-redundant: two physically separate cables (Bus A and Bus B) carry identical traffic. The Bus Controller normally uses Bus A and retries on Bus B if it detects errors. Data rate stays at 1 Mbps on either cable — dual-bus does NOT increase throughput. Commands and responses share the same cable. Only one active Bus Controller is allowed at a time.

practice.question: During a routine flight, a 1553-equipped aircraft experiences chafed wiring that damages one of the two bus cables between the flight computer and an actuator controller. The crew reports no loss of system function. What enabled the system to keep operating?
choices: The Bus Controller automatically doubled the data rate on the surviving cable
The Bus Controller detected errors on the damaged bus and continued traffic on the redundant bus
The Remote Terminal routed commands through an alternate protocol on a different wire
The actuator controller reverted to analog control over its discrete signal lines
practice.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 runs two parallel cables (Bus A and Bus B). When the Bus Controller detects errors on one, it transparently retries on the other — the crew sees no loss of function. Data rate does not change. Remote Terminals do not switch protocols; they simply respond on whichever bus the BC uses. Many 1553-equipped actuators do not have analog fallback wiring.

preassessment.question: Why does MIL-STD-1553 use two parallel bus cables?
choices: To double the data rate
For redundancy if one cable fails
To isolate commands from responses
To support two Bus Controllers

questions.learningObjective: Identify RS-232 as a point-to-point standard limited to two devices per link
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that RS-232 connects exactly two devices per physical link, and that connecting three or more devices requires either multiple RS-232 links, a multi-drop standard such as RS-485, or a gateway / multiplexer.

flashcard.definition: RS-232 is strictly POINT-TO-POINT: one physical link carries a conversation between exactly TWO devices — typically a DTE (Data Terminal Equipment) and a DCE (Data Communication Equipment). It cannot be multi-dropped. To connect three or more devices you need separate RS-232 links for each pair, OR a multi-drop standard like RS-485 or RS-422, OR a gateway/multiplexer that fans out to several RS-232 ports.
flashcard.analogy: RS-232 is a private phone line between two people. If you want to add a third person, you need a three-way calling service (gateway) or each pair needs its own separate line. You cannot just tap the third person onto the existing wires.

jeopardy.question: This limitation of RS-232 makes it impossible to simply tap a third device onto an existing GPS-to-FMS serial link.
choices: The need for a bus controller that RS-232 does not include
A required 120-ohm terminator that most RS-232 devices lack
RS-232 is point-to-point, supporting only two devices per link
Insufficient data rate to carry GPS data at the aircraft's update rate
jeopardy.explanation: RS-232 is strictly point-to-point — one link connects exactly two devices. Tapping a third device on the same pair does not work. To add a third you need a second RS-232 link, a multi-drop standard like RS-485, or a gateway that fans out. RS-232 has no bus controller, no 120-ohm terminator, and more than enough speed for GPS data.

multipleChoice.question: A technician wants to connect three devices — a GPS receiver, a flight management system, and a maintenance laptop — on one RS-232 link so they can all share data. Why will this plan not work as drawn?
choices: RS-232 requires a dedicated bus controller that none of the three devices provides
RS-232 needs 120-ohm termination resistors that these devices do not supply
RS-232 is a point-to-point standard that supports only two devices per link
RS-232 is too slow to carry GPS data at the aircraft's update rate
multipleChoice.explanation: RS-232 is strictly point-to-point — one physical link connects exactly two devices. To connect three you need either a second RS-232 link, a multi-drop standard like RS-485 or RS-422, or a gateway device that fans out to separate RS-232 ports. RS-232 has no bus controller concept, does not require 120-ohm termination (that is CAN bus), and is fast enough for GPS NMEA data.

practice.question: A shop needs to simultaneously feed GPS position data to both the primary FMS and a backup MFD. The GPS receiver has a single RS-232 output. What is the correct approach?
choices: Tap the backup MFD onto the existing RS-232 pair between GPS and FMS using a Y-splitter
Install a 120-ohm terminator on the MFD end to prevent signal reflections on the shared link
Use an RS-232 data splitter or a standard RS-422/485 conversion to drive both receivers
Reconfigure the GPS to transmit ARINC 429 so two devices can receive in parallel
practice.explanation: RS-232 is point-to-point and cannot be tap-spliced to multiple receivers without signal degradation. The correct solutions are: (a) an RS-232 data splitter (a buffered fan-out box that regenerates the signal to multiple outputs), or (b) converting to RS-422/485 which natively supports multi-drop. A 120-ohm terminator is a CAN bus concept, not RS-232. Reconfiguring a GPS to transmit ARINC 429 requires STC-level hardware changes most GPS receivers cannot do.

preassessment.question: How many devices can be connected on a single RS-232 link?
choices: Up to 20, like ARINC 429
Up to 31 plus a Bus Controller, like MIL-STD-1553
Exactly 2 — RS-232 is point-to-point
Unlimited, with proper termination

questions.learningObjective: Explain the purpose of the parity bit in digital databus words
questions.fullObjective: Understand that ARINC 429 and MIL-STD-1553 append a parity bit to each word so the receiver can detect single-bit errors introduced by noise or EMI during transmission; parity detects errors but does not correct them.

flashcard.definition: A single extra bit appended to a digital word so that the total count of '1' bits (including the parity bit) matches a chosen rule — ODD parity for ARINC 429, ODD parity for MIL-STD-1553. The receiver recounts the 1's; if the count does not match the rule, at least one bit flipped during transmission and the word is discarded. Parity DETECTS single-bit errors; it does NOT correct them.
flashcard.analogy: Parity is like counting the chairs before and after a meeting. If the count changes, you know something happened — but the count alone does not tell you WHICH chair was moved. To correct the error, the sender has to resend.

jeopardy.question: This is what the parity bit in an ARINC 429 or MIL-STD-1553 word lets the receiver do.
choices: Synchronize the receiver's clock to the transmitter's bit rate
Identify which transmitter on the bus originated the word
Measure the data rate the transmitter is currently using
Detect whether a bit was corrupted during transmission
jeopardy.explanation: Parity is a single-bit error-detection technique. ARINC 429 uses ODD parity — the parity bit is set so the total number of '1' bits in the 32-bit word is odd. If the receiver counts an EVEN number of 1's, at least one bit flipped and the word is dropped. Parity does NOT synchronize clocks (Manchester encoding does that for 1553), does NOT identify sources (the Label/RT address does), and does NOT measure rates.

multipleChoice.question: An ARINC 429 word includes a parity bit at the end. The receiver counts the '1' bits in the incoming word and compares the total against the parity bit rule. What is the purpose of this check?
choices: To synchronize the receiver's clock to the transmitter
To identify which transmitter originated the word
To measure the current data rate of the transmitter
To detect whether a bit was corrupted during transmission
multipleChoice.explanation: Parity is a simple error-DETECTION technique. ARINC 429 uses ODD parity — the total number of '1' bits in the 32-bit word (including the parity bit) must be odd. If the receiver counts an even number, a bit flipped during transmission (noise, EMI, damaged shield, etc.) and the word is dropped. Parity detects but does NOT correct errors — repeated parity failures typically mean wiring or EMI trouble.

practice.question: An MFD reports occasional stale altitude readings. A databus analyzer on the ARINC 429 line shows a steady flow of valid words, but about 2 percent of them fail the parity check and are dropped by the MFD. What is the most likely root cause?
choices: The transmitter is running at the wrong data rate for that parameter
The MFD software has a bug in its Label filter
The transmitter is broadcasting the wrong Label for altitude
Electrical noise or EMI is corrupting individual bits during transmission
practice.explanation: Parity failures on a small percentage of words point squarely at single-bit corruption during transmission — classic symptoms of EMI pickup, damaged shield, loose shield ground, or noise coupling from nearby high-current wiring. A wrong data rate would cause ALL words to fail, not 2%. A Label-filter bug would affect software behavior without creating parity errors. A wrong Label would deliver data at the wrong time but without parity failures.

preassessment.question: What does a parity bit do in a digital databus word?
choices: Synchronizes the receiver's clock
Identifies the transmitter
Sets the data rate
Detects single-bit transmission errors

questions.learningObjective: Troubleshoot intermittent MIL-STD-1553 bit errors caused by missing or damaged termination
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that MIL-STD-1553 requires 78-ohm terminators at each end of the main bus cable; missing or damaged terminators cause signal reflections that show up as 'ringing' on a bus analyzer and produce intermittent bit errors, often appearing after maintenance in the avionics bay.

flashcard.definition: MIL-STD-1553 requires a 78-ohm resistor at EACH END of the main bus cable. The terminator absorbs the signal when it reaches the end of the cable; without it, the signal REFLECTS back down the cable like a wave hitting a wall. The reflection collides with newly-transmitted waveforms, producing 'ringing' on a scope and bit errors at receivers. Intermittent 1553 errors that appear right after avionics-bay maintenance are the classic symptom of a loose, damaged, or forgotten terminator.
flashcard.analogy: Think of the bus as a long pool. Drop a ball in and it creates a wave. Put a soft sponge at the end (terminator) and the wave is absorbed. Put a hard wall at the end (no terminator) and the wave bounces back, colliding with new waves and turning the pool into chaos.

jeopardy.question: A 1553 bus that worked for months begins showing intermittent bit errors after an avionics-bay inspection. A bus analyzer shows 'ringing' on the waveform. This is the most likely cause.
choices: A 78-ohm terminator has come loose or open-circuited
The Bus Controller software version is out of date
The Remote Terminals are drawing excessive bus current
The 1 Mbps data rate has drifted outside spec
jeopardy.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 requires a 78-ohm terminator at each end of the main cable. A missing or damaged terminator causes the signal to reflect off the end and collide with new transmissions — exactly the 'ringing' seen on an analyzer. Onset right after maintenance points to a terminator that was disturbed. BC software, RT current draw, and data-rate drift produce different symptoms, not ringing.

multipleChoice.question: A MIL-STD-1553 bus that ran error-free for months begins producing intermittent bit errors right after maintenance in the avionics bay. A bus analyzer shows 'ringing' on the waveform. Which fault is most likely responsible?
choices: One of the 78-ohm terminators has come loose or open-circuited
The Bus Controller's software version is out of date
The Remote Terminals are drawing too much current from the bus
The 1 Mbps data rate has drifted outside specification
multipleChoice.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 requires a 78-ohm terminator at EACH END of the main cable. Without proper termination, the signal reflects off the end of the cable, collides with the next transmission, and produces 'ringing' on the scope plus intermittent bit errors. New errors right after avionics-bay work are the classic signature of a disturbed terminator. BC software issues produce protocol faults, excessive RT current produces voltage sag, and data-rate drift produces framing errors — none cause ringing.

practice.question: A tech is called to investigate a MIL-STD-1553 fault that started the day after a scheduled avionics-bay inspection. The symptoms are: scattered bit errors across multiple Remote Terminals, a visible 'ringing' pattern on the bus analyzer's scope trace, and no change in data rate. Where should the tech look first?
choices: At both 78-ohm terminators on the main bus cable for loose or damaged connections
In the Bus Controller's firmware log for a recent software update
At each Remote Terminal's input stage for high bus-current draw
At the overall bus voltage for deviation from the 1 Mbps specification
practice.explanation: Ringing on the waveform plus onset-right-after-maintenance is the textbook signature of a terminator fault. The first thing to check is the two 78-ohm terminators on the main bus cable — they are the most physically exposed component during bay work and the easiest to disturb. BC firmware, RT input stages, and bus voltage are all valid checks, but they do not cause ringing and should come later in the troubleshooting flow.

preassessment.question: What value resistor terminates each end of a MIL-STD-1553 main bus cable?
choices: 78 ohms
50 ohms
120 ohms
600 ohms

questions.learningObjective: Classify an installation as major or minor when a TSO article is installed under an STC that introduces an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an installation performed under a Supplemental Type Certificate which requires an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement is, by its very nature, a major alteration because it changes operating limitations, procedures, or performance information presented to the flight crew.

flashcard.definition: An installation performed under a Supplemental Type Certificate that includes an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement is considered a major alteration, because the AFMS changes the approved operating limitations or procedures of the aircraft.
flashcard.analogy: Think of the AFMS as a new chapter added to the pilot's rulebook; anything that rewrites the rulebook is always a major change, never a minor tweak.

jeopardy.question: This classification applies to an installation approved by an STC whose paperwork includes an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement.
choices: Minor alteration
Preventive maintenance
Major alteration
Minor repair
jeopardy.explanation: Any STC installation that carries an AFMS alters the approved operating limitations or flight procedures, which by definition makes it a major alteration per Part 43 Appendix A.

multipleChoice.question: An avionics shop installs a TSO-approved unit using a Supplemental Type Certificate that is issued with an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement. How should this work be classified?
choices: A minor alteration, because the hardware is TSO approved
Preventive maintenance, because the STC already covers airworthiness
A major alteration, because the STC package changes approved operating data
A minor repair, because the installation follows manufacturer data
multipleChoice.explanation: Per Part 43 Appendix A, a change that affects operating limitations, flight characteristics, or the flight manual is a major alteration. An STC issued with an AFMS alters approved flight data, so the installation is major.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician at a Part 145 shop is installing a new autopilot under an STC. The STC paperwork directs the operator to insert an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement covering the new operating limitations. How should the job be documented and approved?
choices: As preventive maintenance, with a pilot sign-off
As a minor alteration, logged in the airframe record only
As a major alteration requiring FAA Form 337
As a minor repair, since the STC already provides approval
practice.explanation: An STC installation that includes an AFMS changes approved flight data and is therefore a major alteration. Major alterations must be documented on FAA Form 337 in accordance with Part 43 Section 43.9 and Appendix B.

preassessment.question: If an STC installation requires an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement, the work is classified as which of the following?
choices: Minor alteration
Minor repair
Preventive maintenance
Major alteration

questions.targetCaetStem: If a TSO part does have an STC for installation and includes a flight manual supplement, is that installation major or minor?

questions.learningObjective: Decide whether a repair station may perform work on an article listed on its capability list when current technical data is not on hand.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 145 Section 145.109 requires a certificated repair station to have the current manufacturer maintenance manual or other approved technical data available when performing work, even if the article is on the capability list; without current data the work may not be performed.

flashcard.definition: Being on the capability list shows a repair station is approved for a class of work, but Part 145 Section 145.109 still requires the current revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual or other approved technical data before any task is performed.
flashcard.analogy: Having a driver's license (capability list) doesn't let you drive without a working car; the current manual is the car that actually takes you down the road.

jeopardy.question: This is required by Section 145.109 before a repair station works on an article, even when the article already appears on the capability list.
choices: A copy of the operator's minimum equipment list
The current revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual
A signed customer work order
A fresh 8130-3 from the vendor
jeopardy.explanation: Section 145.109 requires current technical data, meaning the latest revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual or other FAA-approved data, to be available and used. The capability list alone does not authorize work without that data.

multipleChoice.question: A Part 145 repair station has a particular communications radio listed on its approved capability list, but the most recent revision of the component maintenance manual has not yet been obtained. May the station perform maintenance on that radio?
choices: No, because current technical data is required under Section 145.109
Yes, because the radio is on the capability list
Yes, as long as a previous revision of the manual is on file
No, unless the FAA Principal Inspector grants a one-time waiver
multipleChoice.explanation: Section 145.109 requires the repair station to have and use the current revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual or other approved data. Capability list coverage does not waive that requirement, and older revisions do not satisfy it.

practice.question: Scenario: A customer brings a VHF communications radio to your Part 145 shop for repair. Your capability list covers the unit, but the manufacturer issued a new manual revision last month and your shop has not downloaded it. What is the correct action?
choices: Proceed using the older manual revision since the radio is on the capability list
Release the unit after bench testing even without the new manual
Obtain the current manual revision before starting the repair
Have the technician use personal notes from a recent training class
practice.explanation: Part 145 Section 145.109 requires the station to maintain and use the current manufacturer maintenance manual. The correct action is to obtain the latest revision before beginning work; neither an older revision nor personal notes meets the regulatory requirement.

preassessment.question: A repair station lists an avionics unit on its capability list, but the newest manual revision has not arrived. Can it perform the repair?
choices: No, current technical data is required
Yes, but only bench testing
Yes, the capability list is enough
Yes, if the customer signs a waiver

questions.targetCaetStem: A repair station’s capability list shows a certain radio as “within capabilities,” but the latest manual revision is not available. Can you perform maintenance on it?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the primary industry-accepted wiring standard that a Part 145 avionics shop follows for installation workmanship.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that FAA Advisory Circular AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 (and for new aircraft, AC 43.13-2B) is the most widely accepted data for acceptable methods, techniques, and practices for avionics wiring installation when no specific manufacturer data applies.

flashcard.definition: The FAA Advisory Circular chapter that is the primary acceptable methods, techniques, and practices for aircraft electrical wiring installation, used by Part 145 shops when the manufacturer's instructions do not cover the specific installation.
flashcard.analogy: It is the electrician's 'building code' for airplanes. Follow it and your wiring is presumed airworthy; ignore it and you own every problem that results.

jeopardy.question: This FAA document is the primary acceptable-methods reference for avionics wiring installation workmanship in a Part 145 shop.
choices: RTCA DO-178C
MIL-STD-810
AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11
TSO-C91
jeopardy.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 provides the FAA-accepted methods, techniques, and practices for aircraft electrical wiring installation, including routing, support, and protection. DO-178C covers software, MIL-STD-810 covers environmental test, and TSO-C91 is an ELT TSO.

multipleChoice.question: Which reference is considered the most important standard to follow when a Part 145 shop routes and supports new avionics wiring and no specific manufacturer installation data applies?
choices: RTCA DO-160 environmental conditions
MIL-STD-704 aircraft power standards
AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 wiring practices
TSO-C126 for 406 MHz ELTs
multipleChoice.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 gives the FAA's accepted methods for aircraft electrical wiring installation and is the default workmanship standard when the aircraft or STC installation instructions do not address a specific detail. The other documents address power quality, environmental qualification, and specific equipment categories.

practice.question: Scenario: You are routing and clamping new wiring for a transponder upgrade in a Part 145 shop. The STC installation instructions do not specify support spacing or bend-radius details. Which document should you follow for the workmanship standard?
choices: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 for wiring practices
MIL-STD-704 for aircraft power limits
RTCA DO-160 for environmental qualification
The operator's Minimum Equipment List
practice.explanation: When an STC or manufacturer document is silent on a wiring workmanship detail, AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 is the FAA-accepted source for installation methods. The other documents address different aspects of airworthiness and do not cover wiring workmanship.

preassessment.question: Which standard is most important for avionics wiring installation in a Part 145 shop?
choices: MIL-STD-704
AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11
RTCA DO-160
TSO-C126

questions.targetCaetStem: When installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 shop, which standard is most crucial?

questions.learningObjective: Identify which party in a Part 145 repair station is ultimately responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 145 places ultimate responsibility on the repair station (through its accountable manager) to ensure current manufacturer and regulatory technical data is available and actually used by personnel performing maintenance.

flashcard.definition: Under Part 145, the repair station itself is responsible for making sure current manufacturer maintenance manuals, service bulletins, and regulatory data are available and used. The accountable manager carries this duty on behalf of the certificate holder.
flashcard.analogy: The certificate holder owns the shop's quality, just like a restaurant owner owns food safety even when line cooks do the cooking.

jeopardy.question: Part 145 assigns ultimate accountability for ensuring technicians have and use current technical data to this role.
choices: The individual technician at the bench
The repair station (through its accountable manager)
The customer who owns the aircraft
The FAA Principal Avionics Inspector
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 Section 145.109 and 145.211 make the repair station, acting through its accountable manager and quality system, responsible for maintaining and using current technical data. The technician follows that data, the customer is not responsible, and the FAA inspector oversees compliance rather than supplies the data.

multipleChoice.question: In a Part 145 avionics repair station, who bears the ultimate responsibility for ensuring that current manufacturer manuals and other approved technical data are on hand and actually used during maintenance?
choices: The individual bench technician
The assigned FAA inspector
The customer who signs the work order
The repair station, through its accountable manager
multipleChoice.explanation: Under Part 145, Sections 145.109 and 145.211 place responsibility for current technical data on the repair station and its accountable manager. The technician has a duty to follow the data, but overall responsibility rests with the certificate holder.

practice.question: Scenario: An FAA inspector visits your Part 145 shop and finds that one bench has an outdated manual. The inspector asks who is responsible for current technical data being on hand and used. Which answer is correct?
choices: The technician, because they do the work
The repair station, through the accountable manager
The customer, because they requested the repair
The FAA, because it issued the certificate
practice.explanation: Responsibility for current technical data rests with the certificate holder under Part 145. The accountable manager ensures the quality system keeps manuals current. Individual technicians must follow the data but do not own the obligation to supply it.

preassessment.question: Under Part 145, who is ultimately responsible for ensuring current technical data is available during maintenance?
choices: The repair station
The customer
The FAA inspector
The bench technician

questions.targetCaetStem: Under Part 145, who is responsible for ensuring that the current technical data is available and used during avionics maintenance?

questions.learningObjective: Describe the scope of a Part 145 repair station's capability list and how it is limited by ratings and approved data.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a repair station's capability list identifies the specific makes and models (within the shop's ratings) that the station is authorized to maintain, provided it has the required housing, equipment, personnel, and current technical data for each listed article.

flashcard.definition: A document that identifies the specific articles a Part 145 repair station is authorized to maintain within its approved ratings. Each entry is backed by a self-evaluation showing the shop has the housing, tools, personnel, and current data required for that article.
flashcard.analogy: Think of it as the menu at a restaurant: the ratings are the cuisines you can cook (Italian, sushi), and the capability list is every specific dish you are actually equipped to serve.

jeopardy.question: This list, maintained under Part 145, identifies the specific articles a repair station is authorized to maintain within its approved ratings.
choices: The shop's tool calibration log
The repair station's capability list
The operator's Minimum Equipment List
The FAA Approved Aircraft Inspection Program
jeopardy.explanation: Under Section 145.215, a limited-rated repair station uses a capability list to identify specific articles it is authorized to maintain. Each addition is backed by a self-evaluation demonstrating compliance with Part 145. The other documents serve unrelated purposes.

multipleChoice.question: How is the authorized work scope of a Part 145 repair station most accurately captured in its capability list?
choices: It lists specific articles the station is authorized to maintain within its approved ratings
It replaces the need for manufacturer maintenance manuals on those articles
It lists every aircraft type the shop may return to service in any condition
It grants blanket authority to perform major repairs on any airframe
multipleChoice.explanation: Per Section 145.215, the capability list names the specific makes and models a limited-rated station may maintain inside its ratings. It does not replace manuals, grant airframe authority, or exempt the station from other Part 145 requirements.

practice.question: Scenario: A new avionics technician asks you what their Part 145 shop's capability list actually allows. Which response best explains the scope?
choices: It names the specific articles the shop may maintain within its ratings, provided required data and resources exist
It replaces the need for service bulletins and manufacturer manuals
It grants temporary authority to return major airframe repairs to service
It permits any work on any aircraft the customer brings in
practice.explanation: The capability list under Section 145.215 identifies specific articles a limited-rated station may maintain, conditioned on proper ratings, facilities, personnel, and current data. It does not replace manuals or extend authority beyond the ratings on the certificate.

preassessment.question: Which best describes a Part 145 repair station's capability list?
choices: Specific articles the shop may maintain within its ratings
A list of all aircraft types the shop can sign off
A substitute for manufacturer manuals
Authority to perform airframe major repairs

questions.targetCaetStem: Which statement best describes the scope of a repair station’s capabilities under Part 145?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3, the Authorized Release Certificate / Airworthiness Approval Tag.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that FAA Form 8130-3 certifies that a specific article (new or overhauled) conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation, providing airworthiness approval for return to service or installation.

flashcard.definition: The Authorized Release Certificate used to provide airworthiness approval for an aircraft part or article, certifying that the item conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation.
flashcard.analogy: It is a birth certificate plus a clean bill of health for a part: it proves the part is both genuine and airworthy at the moment it leaves the issuer.

jeopardy.question: This is the main reason the FAA issues Form 8130-3 with a new or overhauled component.
choices: To document the component warranty terms
To transfer ownership of the article
To list every prior operator of the article
To certify airworthiness approval of the article for installation or return to service
jeopardy.explanation: Form 8130-3 is the Authorized Release Certificate and Airworthiness Approval Tag. Its primary purpose is to certify that the article conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation. It is not a bill of sale, ownership record, or warranty document.

multipleChoice.question: Which statement best captures the main purpose of FAA Form 8130-3, the Authorized Release Certificate?
choices: To transfer legal ownership of an aircraft part between parties
To record pilot training on newly installed avionics
To provide airworthiness approval for an article for installation or return to service
To replace the maintenance record entry for minor repairs
multipleChoice.explanation: Form 8130-3 provides airworthiness approval by certifying that the listed article conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation. It does not convey ownership, pilot training, or replace logbook entries.

practice.question: Scenario: A vendor ships your Part 145 shop an overhauled transponder accompanied by an FAA Form 8130-3. When you review the tag, what does that 8130-3 primarily certify about the unit?
choices: That the unit has been legally sold to your shop
That the unit conforms to approved data and is in a condition for safe operation
That the unit meets a specific warranty duration
That the unit has been flight tested in an aircraft
practice.explanation: The 8130-3 is an airworthiness approval tag certifying conformity to approved design data and condition for safe operation. It is not a bill of sale, warranty document, or flight test record.

preassessment.question: What is the main purpose of FAA Form 8130-3?
choices: Certify airworthiness approval of an article
Transfer part ownership
Record pilot training
Document a warranty period

questions.targetCaetStem: What is the primary purpose of an FAA Form 8130-3 (Airworthiness Approval Tag)?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the recommended practice for inserting and extracting terminated wires from a standard circular connector in a Part 145 shop.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a calibrated insertion/extraction tool designed for the specific connector series must be used to seat and remove terminated contacts, preventing damage to grommets, retention clips, and contacts.

flashcard.definition: A color-coded, series-specific hand tool used to push terminated contacts into, and remove them from, a circular connector without damaging the grommet seal or retention clip.
flashcard.analogy: Like using a proper dental tool instead of a pocketknife: the right instrument protects the delicate surrounding structure while doing the actual work.

jeopardy.question: This is the recommended way to seat or remove terminated contacts in a standard circular connector.
choices: Use the correct color-coded insertion/extraction tool
Push the contact in by hand and pull with the wire
Heat the grommet with a heat gun before inserting
Use needle-nose pliers on the contact barrel
jeopardy.explanation: Only the connector-specific, color-coded insertion/extraction tool seats contacts to the correct depth and releases retention clips without tearing the grommet. Pliers, bare hands, or heat all risk connector damage.

multipleChoice.question: When installing or removing a terminated wire in a standard circular connector at a Part 145 avionics shop, which practice is recommended?
choices: Use needle-nose pliers to grip the contact and push it into place
Use the correct color-coded insertion/extraction tool for that connector series
Pull on the wire itself to extract a contact from the grommet
Warm the grommet with a heat gun until the contact slides freely
multipleChoice.explanation: The accepted practice, reflected in AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 and connector manufacturer data, is to use the series-specific insertion/extraction tool. It protects the grommet, properly seats the contact, and correctly releases retention clips. The other options commonly damage the connector.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician needs to remove a damaged contact from a circular connector on a panel harness and install a new terminated wire in its place. Which approach follows Part 145 best practice?
choices: Use the manufacturer's matching color-coded insertion/extraction tool
Gently pull the wire until the contact pops free of the grommet
Grip the contact shoulder with small pliers and rock it out
Apply heat to soften the grommet, then pull the wire
practice.explanation: The series-specific insertion/extraction tool is the only method that reliably releases the retention clip and preserves the grommet seal. Pulling on the wire, using pliers, or heating the grommet introduces damage that can lead to intermittent connections or loss of environmental seal.

preassessment.question: What is the recommended way to insert or remove a terminated wire in a circular connector?
choices: Use the matching insertion/extraction tool
Pull on the wire
Use needle-nose pliers
Heat the grommet first

questions.targetCaetStem: When inserting or extracting terminated wires from a standard circular connector, which practice is recommended in a Part 145 shop?

questions.learningObjective: Recall the 14 CFR Part 1 definition of maintenance.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that 14 CFR Part 1.1 defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts, but excludes preventive maintenance.

flashcard.definition: Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts, but not preventive maintenance.
flashcard.analogy: Think of maintenance as the 'big bucket' of everything you do to keep an aircraft airworthy, minus the small pilot-level chores (preventive maintenance) that live in their own bucket.

jeopardy.question: According to 14 CFR Part 1, this term means inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts, but not preventive maintenance.
choices: Alteration
Preventive maintenance
Maintenance
Rebuilding
jeopardy.explanation: Part 1.1 defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and replacement of parts, explicitly excluding preventive maintenance. Alteration, preventive maintenance, and rebuilding each have their own separate definitions.

multipleChoice.question: According to 14 CFR Part 1, which definition best matches the regulatory meaning of maintenance?
choices: Any modification that changes the aircraft's type design
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and replacement of parts, excluding preventive maintenance
Simple preservation and minor tasks a pilot may perform
Only the repair of structural damage to an airframe
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 1.1 defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts, and specifically excludes preventive maintenance. Alterations and pilot-performed preventive tasks are defined separately.

practice.question: Scenario: During a shop orientation, a new technician asks what the regulations actually mean by 'maintenance.' Which answer is correct under 14 CFR Part 1?
choices: Any change to the aircraft's approved type design
Only tasks listed in Appendix A as preventive maintenance
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and replacement of parts, not including preventive maintenance
Any cleaning or cosmetic work performed on the aircraft
practice.explanation: Part 1.1 gives a specific definition of maintenance covering inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and replacement of parts, while excluding preventive maintenance. Cosmetic work, type design changes, and preventive tasks do not match this definition.

preassessment.question: Under 14 CFR Part 1, what is maintenance?
choices: Any type design change
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement
Only structural repair
Any cosmetic cleaning

questions.targetCaetStem: According to FAA regulations, what is the definition of “maintenance”?

questions.learningObjective: Explain why Part 145 shops must keep walkways and work areas clear of clutter.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 145 Section 145.103 requires housing and facilities to support safe work, so keeping walkways and work areas clear prevents personnel injury, protects aircraft and parts, and reduces FOD risk.

flashcard.definition: The discipline of keeping walkways, benches, and storage clear of clutter. Required by Part 145 Section 145.103 and OSHA to prevent slips, trips, falls, FOD events, and damage to aircraft parts.
flashcard.analogy: A cluttered shop is like a crowded staircase: the first misstep produces injuries and broken hardware that were entirely avoidable.

jeopardy.question: This is the main reason Part 145 shops must keep walkways and work areas free of clutter.
choices: To impress the FAA inspector visually
To fit more inventory into a small building
To prevent slips, trips, injuries, and damage to aircraft parts
To meet a customer contract requirement
jeopardy.explanation: Clear walkways prevent slip and trip injuries, reduce foreign object damage, and protect aircraft parts from being struck or dropped. Section 145.103 requires facilities that support safe work, making clutter control a regulatory as well as a safety matter.

multipleChoice.question: At a general aviation repair station operating under Part 145, explain why aisles and benches must be kept uncluttered.
choices: So the shop appears tidy for visiting customers
So additional tooling can be stored on the floor
Because the FAA audits walkway width once per year
To prevent slips, trips, personal injury, and damage to aircraft parts
multipleChoice.explanation: Section 145.103 requires housing and facilities that support safe work. Clear walkways protect personnel from slip and trip injuries, reduce foreign object damage risk, and keep removed parts and aircraft hardware from being damaged. Cosmetics and storage convenience are not the driver.

practice.question: Scenario: A new technician is curious why the lead insists that hoses, cables, and boxes never sit in a walkway overnight. Which answer best captures the regulatory and safety reason?
choices: It makes the shop look better in photos
It reduces the shop's electrical utility bill
It is only a personal preference of the lead technician
It prevents slip, trip, and fall injuries and protects aircraft parts, as Part 145.103 requires safe facilities
practice.explanation: Part 145.103 requires facilities that support safe work. Clear walkways prevent slips, trips, and falls and protect aircraft parts from being damaged by feet, carts, or falling objects. The other answers either ignore the regulation or miss the actual safety rationale.

preassessment.question: Why must a Part 145 shop keep walkways clear of clutter?
choices: To look organized for customers
To prevent injuries and part damage
To store extra inventory
To meet utility requirements

questions.targetCaetStem: In a Part 145 general aviation repair station, why is it crucial to keep walkways and work areas free of clutter?

questions.learningObjective: Calculate total power consumption given voltage and current.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that electrical power in a DC circuit equals the product of voltage and current (P = V x I), and apply this to compute wattage from measured values.

flashcard.term: Power in a 12 V / 2 A circuit
flashcard.definition: 24 watts. P equals voltage multiplied by current, so 12 V x 2 A = 24 W.
flashcard.analogy: Think of voltage as water pressure and current as flow rate. Pressure times flow equals the total 'work' the water can do.

jeopardy.question: This is the power dissipated when 12 volts pushes 2 amps through a load.
jeopardy.explanation: Power equals voltage times current (P = V x I). Multiplying 12 V by 2 A yields 24 watts of total consumption.

multipleChoice.question: How many watts are consumed by a 12-volt DC load drawing 2 amps?
choices: 6 W
multipleChoice.explanation: Use P = V x I. With 12 V and 2 A, the circuit dissipates 24 watts. Dividing (6 W) or adding (14 W) misapplies the formula.

practice.question: Scenario: While troubleshooting a 12-volt aux lighting circuit, your clamp meter reads 2 amps of draw. What total wattage is the circuit consuming?
choices: 6 W
practice.explanation: Power is simply voltage x current. 12 V x 2 A = 24 W. This is the figure you would compare against the bulb or equipment rating to verify normal operation.

preassessment.question: A DC circuit runs on 12 V and draws 2 A. How much power does it consume?

questions.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are troubleshooting a circuit that uses a 12V power supply. You measure the current and find it to be 2. What is the total power consumption of the circuit?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the correct safe procedure for disposing of a Ni-Cad battery.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Ni-Cad batteries contain toxic cadmium and must be handled as hazardous waste by sending them to an authorized recycling facility rather than placing them in general trash.

flashcard.definition: Return to a certified hazardous-waste recycler. Cadmium is toxic, so Ni-Cad cells must never go into regular trash or be incinerated.
flashcard.analogy: Like used engine oil, the battery is useful when contained but poisonous if it leaks into the environment, so it needs a licensed recycler.

jeopardy.question: This is the correct disposal method for a spent aircraft Ni-Cad battery.
choices: Deliver it to a certified hazardous-waste recycler
Short both terminals, then bury it offsite
Incinerate the cells to destroy the electrolyte
Drop it into the shop's general trash dumpster
jeopardy.explanation: Cadmium inside a Ni-Cad is a regulated toxic metal. Federal and local rules require routing the battery to an approved hazardous-waste recycler; trashing, burying, or burning cells is prohibited.

multipleChoice.question: Which method is the approved way to dispose of a used Ni-Cad battery?
choices: Place it in ordinary shop trash
Crush the cells and discard the pieces
Throw it into a fire to destroy the chemicals
Turn it in to a certified hazardous-waste recycler
multipleChoice.explanation: Ni-Cad cells contain cadmium, a regulated toxic metal. Only a certified hazardous-waste recycler may accept them. Trash, crushing, or burning releases hazardous material and violates disposal rules.

practice.question: Scenario: You have just removed a time-expired Ni-Cad battery from a corporate jet. What disposal action complies with hazardous-waste rules?
choices: Toss it into the hangar's roll-off dumpster
Leave it on a hazmat shelf indefinitely
Route it to an authorized hazardous-waste recycler
Discharge it and recycle it with household alkalines
practice.explanation: Regulations require routing cadmium-bearing batteries to an authorized hazardous-waste recycler. Dumpsters, indefinite shelving, or household recycling streams are not approved pathways.

preassessment.question: What is the SAFE way to get rid of a used Ni-Cad battery?
choices: Toss it in general trash
Burn it in a metal drum
Bury it in the field behind the hangar
Recycle through a certified hazardous-waste facility

questions.targetCaetStem: Which of the following is a SAFE procedure for disposing of a Ni-Cad battery?

questions.learningObjective: Determine the total current in a parallel circuit.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that in a parallel circuit the total current supplied by the source equals the sum of the individual branch currents (Kirchhoff's current law).

flashcard.definition: Add every branch current together. It = I1 + I2 + I3 ..., because each path draws independently from the source.
flashcard.analogy: Picture three garden hoses fed by one faucet. Total faucet flow equals the sum of the flow out of each hose.

jeopardy.question: This is how total current is determined when resistors are wired in parallel.
choices: Sum each branch current (I1 + I2 + I3 ...)
Divide source voltage by the smallest resistor
Multiply all branch currents together
Subtract the smallest branch current from the largest
jeopardy.explanation: Kirchhoff's current law states that current entering a junction equals current leaving it. In a parallel network, the source must supply the sum of the individual branch currents.

multipleChoice.question: What method gives the total current flowing from the source in a parallel network?
choices: Divide source voltage by the largest resistor
Average the branch currents
Use the current measured in only one branch
Add the current through each branch together
multipleChoice.explanation: By Kirchhoff's current law, total current in a parallel circuit is the algebraic sum of branch currents. Using a single branch or averaging underestimates the source load.

practice.question: Scenario: A 28 V aircraft bus feeds three parallel loads drawing 2 A, 3 A, and 5 A. How should you determine the current the bus must supply?
choices: Add the three branch currents for 10 A total
Divide 28 V by the total resistance of one branch
Use only the largest load (5 A)
Average the three branch currents
practice.explanation: The bus must deliver the sum of all branch currents: 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 A. Each parallel path draws independently, and the source current equals the total.

preassessment.question: In a parallel DC circuit, total current from the source equals:
choices: The smallest branch current
The sum of every branch current
The product of all branch currents
The difference between two branches

questions.targetCaetStem: In a parallel circuit, how do you find total current?

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose unstable bus voltage within operational limits as a regulator problem.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that slow drift or fluctuation of DC bus voltage inside operational limits typically indicates a marginal voltage regulator rather than battery or load failure.

flashcard.definition: Most commonly points to a marginal voltage regulator. The regulator can still hold within limits but fails to deliver rock-steady output.
flashcard.analogy: Like a thermostat that keeps the room between 68 and 72 but never settles on one temperature.

jeopardy.question: This component is the usual suspect when DC bus voltage wanders but stays within tolerance.
choices: A marginal voltage regulator
A shorted annunciator lamp
The battery main contactor
The landing-gear squat switch
jeopardy.explanation: A deteriorating voltage regulator often holds output inside its spec band but can no longer maintain it steady. Contactors, lamps, and squat switches do not control bus voltage.

multipleChoice.question: Which defect best explains bus voltage that fluctuates yet stays inside operational limits?
choices: A deteriorating voltage regulator
An open circuit breaker on the main bus
A deeply discharged battery
A corroded airframe ground
multipleChoice.explanation: A failing regulator still controls voltage loosely, producing in-band drift. A dead battery or open CB would drop the bus out of limits, and a bad ground usually shows as a voltage drop under load rather than continuous wandering.

practice.question: Scenario: An aircraft's avionics panel shows power fluctuations. Your voltmeter on the main bus reads unstable but within operational limits. Which component most likely needs attention?
choices: The voltage regulator
The battery relay
The pitot heat circuit breaker
The static inverter output transformer
practice.explanation: In-band wandering is classic voltage-regulator behavior. The regulator still acts on the field but no longer corrects precisely. Battery relays cause on/off symptoms, and unrelated breakers or inverters would not drive subtle bus drift.

preassessment.question: Bus voltage drifts but stays in limits. Most likely cause?
choices: Marginal voltage regulator
Shorted busbar
Open battery fuse
Low generator RPM only

questions.targetCaetStem: Scenario: An aircraft's avionics system is showing power fluctuations. Upon inspecting the power supply, you find the voltage to be unstable but within operational limits. What is the most likely cause of the issue?

questions.learningObjective: Predict how current changes when voltage doubles at constant resistance.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that by Ohm's Law (I = V/R), current is directly proportional to voltage, so doubling the applied voltage doubles the current when resistance is held constant.

flashcard.definition: Current doubles. I = V/R, so current rises in direct proportion to voltage when R does not change.
flashcard.analogy: Like opening the valve twice as wide at the water tank while the pipe stays the same - flow exactly doubles.

jeopardy.question: This is what happens to current when applied voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged.
choices: It is cut in half
It quadruples
It stays exactly the same
It doubles
jeopardy.explanation: Ohm's Law says I = V/R. With R fixed, doubling V doubles I. Halving or quadrupling would require resistance to change or power (I-squared) to be the variable.

multipleChoice.question: If applied voltage is doubled while resistance remains constant, the circuit current will:
choices: Double
Remain unchanged
Drop to one-half its former value
Increase by a factor of four
multipleChoice.explanation: Ohm's Law is linear: I = V/R. Doubling V while R is constant exactly doubles I. Quadrupling applies to power (P = V^2/R), not current.

practice.question: Scenario: A test bench normally runs at 14 V through a fixed load. You switch the supply to 28 V without changing the load resistance. What happens to current?
choices: It doubles
It stays the same
It is halved
It increases by four times
practice.explanation: Because resistance is unchanged and voltage doubled (14 V to 28 V), current also doubles per Ohm's Law. Overlooking this is how techs blow fuses when bench-testing 14 V equipment on a 28 V supply.

preassessment.question: Double the voltage, hold resistance the same: current will:
choices: Halve
Stay the same
Double
Quadruple

questions.targetCaetStem: What will happen to the current in a circuit if the voltage doubles while the resistance stays the same?

questions.learningObjective: Calculate total resistance of three resistors in parallel.
questions.fullObjective: Apply the parallel resistance formula 1/Rt = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3 to compute an equivalent resistance that is always smaller than the smallest branch.

flashcard.definition: About 323 ohms. Use 1/Rt = 1/10000 + 1/1000 + 1/500; invert the sum to get Rt.
flashcard.analogy: Adding more pipes between two tanks; each new pipe lowers total resistance to flow.

jeopardy.question: This is the total resistance of 10k, 1k, and 500 ohms placed in parallel.
choices: About 11,500 ohms
About 3,833 ohms
About 167 ohms
About 323 ohms
jeopardy.explanation: 1/Rt = 1/10000 + 1/1000 + 1/500 = 0.0031; Rt is about 323 ohms. Total parallel resistance is always less than the smallest branch (500 ohms), so 323 ohms fits.

multipleChoice.question: Three resistors (10,000 ohms, 1,000 ohms, and 500 ohms) are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance?
choices: About 11,500 ohms
About 3,833 ohms
About 323 ohms
About 167 ohms
multipleChoice.explanation: Sum the reciprocals: 0.0001 + 0.001 + 0.002 = 0.0031. Invert to get Rt about 323 ohms. Option 11,500 is the series sum, and 3,833 is the mean - both misuses of parallel math.

practice.question: Scenario: On a schematic you see three parallel resistors labeled 10k ohms, 1k ohms, and 500 ohms. What is the total resistance between the common nodes?
choices: About 11,500 ohms (series total)
About 323 ohms (parallel total)
About 3,833 ohms (arithmetic mean)
About 167 ohms (one-third of smallest)
practice.explanation: Use 1/Rt = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3. Solving gives Rt about 323 ohms, which is correctly less than 500 ohms (the smallest branch). Series addition or averaging does not apply to parallel networks.

preassessment.question: Three parallel resistors of 10k, 1k, and 500 ohms yield a total resistance of about:
choices: 11,500 ohms
3,800 ohms
323 ohms
50 ohms

questions.targetCaetStem: Scenario: In a parallel circuit with three resistors 10k ohms, 1k ohms, and 500 ohms), what is the total resistance?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the component used to suppress electrical noise from an alternator.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a capacitor (often called an alternator noise filter) placed across the output shunts high-frequency ripple and brush noise to ground, cleaning up avionics power.

flashcard.definition: A capacitor tied from the alternator B-lead to ground. It shunts AC ripple and brush noise to structure while letting DC pass.
flashcard.analogy: Like a surge tank on a water line - smooths pressure spikes without blocking steady flow.

jeopardy.question: This component, tied from an alternator's B-lead to ground, filters whine and ripple.
choices: A bypass capacitor
A carbon resistor
A series inductor
A power diode
jeopardy.explanation: A bypass capacitor offers low impedance to AC noise, shunting alternator ripple to ground while passing DC undisturbed. Diodes rectify, resistors waste energy, and a plain series inductor would choke DC too.

multipleChoice.question: Which device is most commonly installed to suppress noise produced by an alternator?
choices: A solid-state relay
A wire-wound resistor
A power rectifier diode
A bypass capacitor to ground
multipleChoice.explanation: Bypass capacitors absorb high-frequency ripple and brush hash, providing a low-impedance path to ground for AC while leaving the DC output unaffected. Other parts listed do not filter ripple.

practice.question: Scenario: Pilots report alternator whine in the intercom. Which component should you add or verify to quiet the alternator's electrical noise?
choices: A fuse on the field lead
A bypass capacitor at the B-terminal
A crowbar diode on the bus
A shunt resistor across the battery
practice.explanation: The classic fix for alternator whine is a bypass capacitor at the output terminal to structural ground. It shorts AC ripple to ground while passing DC. Fuses, crowbar diodes, and shunt resistors do not filter noise.

preassessment.question: To suppress noise coming from an alternator, techs usually install a:
choices: Capacitor
Resistor
Diode
Transformer

questions.targetCaetStem: Which of the following components would you typically use to suppress noise in an alternator?

questions.learningObjective: Compute power dissipation on a 28 V DC bus with a given current.
questions.fullObjective: Apply P = V x I to determine the wattage consumed on a 28 V bus drawing 25 A, recognizing standard aircraft bus arithmetic.

flashcard.definition: 700 watts. P = V x I = 28 x 25.
flashcard.analogy: Like multiplying pressure (V) by flow (I) on a hydraulic system to get total work output.

jeopardy.question: This is the power drawn when a 28 V DC bus supplies 25 amps.
jeopardy.explanation: P = V x I = 28 V x 25 A = 700 W. Adding instead of multiplying gives 53 W, and dividing or doubling produces the other distractors.

multipleChoice.question: What power is dissipated by a 28-volt DC bus carrying 25 amps of current?
multipleChoice.explanation: Power is voltage times current. 28 V x 25 A yields 700 watts. Other answers come from adding, misplacing a decimal, or doubling.

practice.question: Scenario: On a twin-engine aircraft, the 28-volt DC main bus carries 25 amps during cruise. What total power is being supplied at that moment?
practice.explanation: Use P = V x I: 28 V x 25 A = 700 W. This is the figure technicians compare against bus capacity and alternator output rating to confirm margin.

preassessment.question: A 28 V bus drawing 25 A consumes how much power?

questions.targetCaetStem: In a multi-engine aircraft with a 28?V DC bus drawing 25?A, how much power is consumed?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the algebraic expression for Ohm's Law.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Ohm's Law is written V = I x R (equivalently I = V/R or R = V/I) and select the correct algebraic form from common distractors.

flashcard.definition: V = I x R. Rearranged: I = V/R and R = V/I.
flashcard.analogy: Voltage is the push, resistance is the pipe's narrowness, and current is the flow that results from dividing push by narrowness.

jeopardy.question: This is the algebraic statement of Ohm's Law.
choices: V = I + R
V = I x R
V = I / R
V = R / I
jeopardy.explanation: Ohm's Law says voltage equals current multiplied by resistance. Addition, or dividing current by resistance, are common misstatements.

multipleChoice.question: Which equation states Ohm's Law correctly?
choices: V = I + R
V = I x R
V = I / R
V = R - I
multipleChoice.explanation: V equals I times R. Current through a conductor is proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance, which gives the product form V = IR.

practice.question: Scenario: You are reviewing a trainee's notes and see four candidate equations. Which one is the correct algebraic statement of Ohm's Law?
choices: V = I + R
V = R / I
V = I x R
V = I^2 x R
practice.explanation: Ohm's Law is V = I x R. Adding current and resistance is nonsense dimensionally, and I^2R is the power-dissipation formula, not Ohm's Law.

preassessment.question: Which expression is Ohm's Law?
choices: V = I + R
V = I x R
V = R / I
V = I / R

questions.targetCaetStem: Ohm's Law states that the current (I) through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage (V) and inversely proportional to the resistance (R) Which of the following formulas represents Ohm's Law?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the term for opposition to AC current caused by an inductor.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that inductive reactance (XL, in ohms) describes an inductor's opposition to alternating current and varies directly with frequency (XL = 2 x pi x f x L).

flashcard.definition: Opposition to AC that an inductor presents. Symbol XL, measured in ohms, equals 2 x pi x frequency x inductance.
flashcard.analogy: A spinning flywheel resists sudden changes in direction; an inductor resists sudden changes in current, opposing AC more at higher frequencies.

jeopardy.question: This is the name given to the opposition inductors present to alternating current.
choices: Impedance
Capacitive reactance
Conductance
Inductive reactance
