jeopardy.explanation: Gauge selection is driven by current, voltage drop, and installation environment. The installation manual and AC 43.13-1B tables provide the calculation basis. Copying a different circuit or defaulting to the thinnest wire invites overload and excessive voltage drop.

multipleChoice.question: A new avionics install requires a specific wire gauge. Which evaluation is the one the technician absolutely cannot skip?
choices: Select the thinnest wire that fits the connector contacts
Match the gauge of the existing cabin-lighting wiring in the aircraft
Copy the wire gauge used on a different aircraft make and model
Calculate the required gauge from the equipment's current draw, acceptable voltage drop over the run, and operating environment
multipleChoice.explanation: Gauge is a calculated value: current, voltage drop, and environment drive the answer. AC 43.13-1B provides selection tables, and the installation manual lists specific requirements. Copying unrelated circuits or minimizing to connector size can produce overheating or excessive voltage drop.

practice.question: A technician is installing a new 10 A avionics unit 20 feet from the bus. Which step is most critical for correct wire gauge selection?
choices: Copy the gauge from a similar-looking circuit on another aircraft model
Select the smallest wire that will fit into the equipment's connector pins
Calculate the required gauge from the 10 A current draw, the acceptable voltage drop over the 20-foot run, and the operating environment (bundle vs free-air)
Use the gauge of the cabin lighting wiring already in the aircraft
practice.explanation: Wire gauge is calculated from current, run length (voltage drop), and environment. AC 43.13-1B tables and the installation manual provide the values. Copying unrelated circuits or fitting to the connector can produce overheating or unacceptable voltage drop.

preassessment.question: What is the critical step in selecting wire gauge for new avionics?
choices: Match another aircraft's wiring
Use the thinnest wire that fits the pins
Use cabin-lighting gauge
Calculate from current draw, voltage drop, and operating environment

questions.targetCaetStem: Which step is critical for selecting the correct wire gauge during a new avionics equipment installation?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the main purpose of strain relief in aircraft wiring
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that strain relief protects the wire-to-contact joint by absorbing mechanical loads — pull, flex, and vibration — before they can reach the termination, preventing pin pull-outs and conductor fatigue failures.

flashcard.definition: Strain relief absorbs mechanical pull, flex, and vibration at the point where the wire exits a connector or termination. It prevents the wire from bending or pulling directly on the pin-to-conductor joint, which would otherwise fatigue and fail.
flashcard.analogy: Strain relief is like a bend radius on a garden hose — it keeps the fitting from seeing the flex that would tear it loose.

jeopardy.question: This is the main reason strain relief is added to aircraft wire terminations.
choices: To absorb pull, flex, and vibration before they reach the wire-to-contact joint
To boost the current rating of individual pins
To color-code the cable by circuit function
To give the cable a decorative appearance at the connector
jeopardy.explanation: Strain relief protects the delicate pin-to-wire joint from mechanical loads that would otherwise fatigue the conductor or pull the pin. It is not decorative, does not change current rating, and does not color-code the circuit.

multipleChoice.question: What is the main reason strain relief is used at aircraft wire terminations?
choices: To raise the current-carrying capacity of each contact
To make the termination look tidy and organized
To absorb pull, flex, and vibration before they can reach the wire-to-contact joint
To provide a color code for each circuit at the connector
multipleChoice.explanation: Strain relief exists to keep mechanical loads from reaching the pin-to-wire joint. Flex at the joint fatigues the conductor; pull can extract the pin. Appearance, current rating, and color coding are not strain-relief functions.

practice.question: A technician notices that a new harness has no strain relief at the rear of a connector. What consequence is most likely if the installation is left as-is?
choices: Flex and vibration at the connector exit will work the wire-to-contact joint, eventually fatiguing conductors or pulling pins out
The connector pins will overheat because strain relief controls current flow
The harness will look untidy but will function correctly
The cable color codes will fade faster
practice.explanation: With no strain relief, flex and vibration act directly on the pin-to-wire junction. Over time this fatigues the conductor or pulls the pin. Strain relief does not control current and does not affect color coding.

preassessment.question: Why is strain relief added to aircraft wiring?
choices: Neat appearance
Raise pin current rating
Absorb pull, flex, and vibration before the wire-to-contact joint
Provide color coding

questions.targetCaetStem: What is the main reason for using strain relief in aircraft wiring?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the recommended stripping practice for Teflon-insulated avionics wire
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Teflon-insulated wire should be stripped with a precision (controlled-depth) mechanical stripper; heat strippers and uncontrolled blade strippers risk nicking the conductor or deforming the insulation, reducing reliability.

flashcard.definition: Teflon (PTFE) insulation is tough and temperature-resistant, but is easy to damage if stripping is uncontrolled. The recommended method is a precision mechanical stripper with the correct blade size for the wire gauge. Heat strippers can char the insulation; general-purpose strippers risk nicking strands.
flashcard.analogy: Stripping Teflon with a heat stripper is like peeling an apple with a blowtorch — the outside comes off, but so does a lot more than you wanted.

jeopardy.question: This is the recommended practice when using a stripping tool on Teflon-coated avionics wire.
choices: Use a hot-blade (thermal) stripper to burn through the insulation
Use a general-purpose multi-gauge stripper at its widest setting
Use a precision controlled-depth mechanical stripper with the correct blade for the wire gauge
Pull the insulation off by hand after scoring it with a knife
jeopardy.explanation: Precision mechanical strippers with the right blade size cleanly remove Teflon without nicking strands. Thermal strippers can damage Teflon and release irritating fumes; wide-setting general-purpose tools are imprecise; knife scoring risks nicking the conductor.

multipleChoice.question: For an avionics install using Teflon-insulated wire, what should a technician observe when operating automatic strippers?
choices: Use a precision controlled-depth mechanical stripper with the correct blade size for the wire gauge
Use a general-purpose stripper at its widest opening
Use a hot-blade (thermal) stripper to melt through the insulation
Score the insulation with a knife and pull it off by hand
multipleChoice.explanation: Teflon is stripped with a precision mechanical stripper matched to the wire gauge. Thermal strippers can degrade Teflon and release fumes; oversized general-purpose tools nick conductors; knife scoring is imprecise and frequently damages strands.

practice.question: A technician is stripping 22-gauge Teflon-insulated wire for a new avionics installation. Which technique best protects the conductor and insulation?
choices: Use a precision mechanical stripper with the 22-gauge blade, verifying no strands are nicked after the cut
Use a general-purpose stripper at its widest setting to clear the insulation quickly
Burn off the insulation with a hot-blade stripper set to maximum temperature
Score the insulation with a pocketknife and pull it off by hand
practice.explanation: Precision mechanical stripping — correct blade size, controlled depth, post-strip inspection — protects both the conductor and the insulation. Thermal stripping can char Teflon; oversize or knife methods are prone to nicking strands, which creates stress risers that fail in service.

preassessment.question: How should Teflon-coated avionics wire be stripped?
choices: With a hot-blade stripper
With a general-purpose stripper at its widest setting
With a precision controlled-depth mechanical stripper matched to the gauge
By scoring with a knife and pulling off by hand

questions.targetCaetStem: Which practice is recommended when using automatic wire strippers on Teflon-coated wires for avionics installations?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the primary purpose of shielded wire in avionics
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that shielded wire is used primarily to reduce electromagnetic interference — both preventing external noise from coupling onto the signal and preventing the signal from radiating outward — by providing a grounded conductive barrier around the signal conductors.

flashcard.definition: Shielded wire reduces electromagnetic coupling. A grounded conductive braid or foil around the signal conductors intercepts external noise that would otherwise couple onto the signal, and also contains radiation from the signal. It is standard for digital data buses and sensitive analog signals.
flashcard.analogy: A shield is a Faraday cage for the wire — outside noise hits the cage and is dumped to ground instead of reaching the conductor.

jeopardy.question: This is the main reason avionics installations use shielded wire on sensitive signal circuits.
choices: To increase the maximum current the wire can carry
To improve the flexibility of the cable
To color-code the wire for circuit identification
To reduce electromagnetic interference by intercepting external noise and containing internal radiation
jeopardy.explanation: Shielding is an EMI control — a grounded conductive layer around the signal conductors. It does not raise current rating, does not improve flexibility (shields typically reduce it), and is not a color code.

multipleChoice.question: On avionics circuits, the PRIMARY reason for choosing shielded wire is to:
choices: Increase the current the conductor can carry
Improve the cable's mechanical flexibility
Reduce electromagnetic interference by intercepting external noise and containing internal radiation
Provide additional color coding for circuit identification
multipleChoice.explanation: Shielded wire exists to attenuate EMI coupling into or out of the signal conductor. Current rating is set by conductor cross-section, not shielding. Shielded cable is generally stiffer, and shielding does not serve as a color code.

practice.question: A technician is choosing cable for an analog sensor signal line that runs close to high-current feeders. Why does the installation manual call for shielded wire?
choices: To raise the permitted current on the sensor circuit
To improve the cable's bend radius and flexibility
To add another layer of color coding for identification
To reduce EMI coupling between the high-current feeders and the sensor signal, so the reading remains accurate
practice.explanation: Near high-current feeders, the sensor signal is vulnerable to inductive and capacitive coupling. A grounded shield attenuates that coupling. Shielding does not raise current rating, does not improve flexibility, and is not a color code.

preassessment.question: Shielded wire is used primarily to:
choices: Raise current rating
Improve flexibility
Reduce EMI coupling
Act as a color code

questions.targetCaetStem: Shielded wire is used in avionics systems PRIMARILY to:

questions.learningObjective: Identify the first item to check when a newly installed avionics system fails to power up
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a newly installed unit that will not power up is checked first for shorts and correct wiring (especially power and ground) before moving on to continuity checks and verification of voltages at the unit — protecting the unit and reducing troubleshooting time.

flashcard.definition: When a newly installed avionics unit does not power up, the first check is the wiring — verify no shorts between power and ground, confirm correct power/ground routing against the installation drawing, and inspect pin seating at the connectors. Voltage-at-unit checks come next.
flashcard.analogy: Check the power side first — if power cannot get in (or is shorting out), nothing else downstream will work.

jeopardy.question: This is the FIRST thing a technician should check when a newly installed avionics system fails to power up.
choices: The firmware version loaded in the unit's memory
The aircraft's paint condition near the antenna
The performance of the on-board GPS receiver
The wiring — looking for shorts, incorrect power/ground routing, or unseated pins
jeopardy.explanation: Power-up failures most often come from wiring errors — a ground-to-power short, a swapped power feed, or an unseated pin. Firmware, GPS performance, and paint are not the first line of investigation.

multipleChoice.question: A technician completes a new avionics installation, but the unit fails to power up on first application of power. Which of the following should be checked FIRST?
choices: The firmware version installed in the unit
The GPS reception at the current location
The condition of the exterior paint near the antenna
The wiring — check for shorts, verify correct power/ground routing against the drawing, and confirm pins are seated
multipleChoice.explanation: New-installation power-up failures are overwhelmingly wiring problems: ground-to-power shorts, reversed power, unseated pins. Wiring verification protects the unit and is the quickest diagnostic. Firmware, GPS, and paint are later-stage concerns.

practice.question: After completing a new avionics install, a technician applies power and the unit remains dark. What is the FIRST diagnostic step?
choices: Reload the unit's firmware from the manufacturer's website
Verify the wiring — check for shorts, confirm correct power and ground per the installation drawing, and inspect pin seating
Inspect the aircraft's exterior paint around antenna cutouts
Evaluate on-board GPS satellite acquisition
practice.explanation: Most new-install power-up failures are wiring-related: a short to ground, a swapped power/ground pair, or an unseated contact. Checking wiring first protects the equipment and isolates the fault quickly. Firmware, GPS, and paint checks do not address the symptom.

preassessment.question: A newly installed avionics unit will not power up. What do you check first?
choices: Wiring for shorts, correct power/ground, and pin seating
GPS reception
Exterior paint
Firmware version

questions.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are tasked with wiring a new avionics system into an aircraft. After completing the installation, the system fails to power up. Which of the following is the FIRST thing you should check?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the best corrective action for a harness with a damaged outer jacket
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a harness with a damaged outer jacket should be evaluated, and either repaired with approved protective sleeving/heat-shrink where underlying conductors are intact, or the damaged section replaced if the conductors are also compromised — per AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11.

flashcard.definition: A harness with a damaged outer jacket is inspected to determine whether underlying conductors are intact. If conductors are undamaged, the jacket is repaired or protected with approved sleeving or heat-shrink. If conductors are damaged, the affected section is replaced per AC 43.13-1B.
flashcard.analogy: A damaged jacket is like a torn coat sleeve — if the arm underneath is fine, patch the coat; if the arm is hurt too, more than a patch is needed.

jeopardy.question: This is the best corrective action when a harness is found with a damaged outer jacket during an inspection.
choices: Ignore the damage if the circuit still functions
Inspect for conductor damage, then repair the jacket with approved sleeving/heat-shrink — or replace the affected section if conductors are compromised
Wrap the damaged area with standard electrical tape and release the aircraft
Cut the entire harness out and replace it in full as a precaution
jeopardy.explanation: The proper action is condition-based: check the conductors, then repair the jacket with approved protection or replace the section if conductors are damaged. Ignoring damage or using tape is not acceptable. Full harness replacement is excessive when localized repair is allowed.

multipleChoice.question: During an inspection, a technician finds a harness with a damaged outer jacket. What is the BEST corrective action?
choices: Ignore the damage because the circuit is still operational
Wrap the damaged area with standard electrical tape and return the aircraft to service
Inspect the conductors, then repair the jacket with approved protective sleeving or heat-shrink — or replace the affected section if conductors are also damaged
Remove and replace the entire harness as a precaution
multipleChoice.explanation: AC 43.13-1B guides a condition-based repair: verify conductor condition, protect/repair the jacket if conductors are intact, or replace the section if they are not. Tape is temporary; ignoring damage is prohibited; full replacement is unnecessary when a section repair is allowed.

practice.question: A technician discovers a 4-inch section of damaged outer jacket on a harness, with no visible conductor damage. What is the best corrective action?
choices: Inspect the conductors to confirm they are undamaged, then repair the jacket with approved protective sleeving or heat-shrink tubing
Wrap the area with electrical tape and release the aircraft
Remove the entire harness and install a brand-new one
Ignore the finding because the circuit still works
practice.explanation: Conductor condition drives the action. Intact conductors + damaged jacket = repair with approved protection. Damaged conductors = replace the affected section. Tape is not an approved repair. Full replacement is excessive for a localized jacket repair.

preassessment.question: A harness has a damaged outer jacket but the conductors look intact. Best action?
choices: Inspect conductors, then repair the jacket with approved sleeving or heat-shrink
Ignore it
Wrap with electrical tape
Replace the entire harness

questions.targetCaetStem: During inspection, you find a harness with a damaged outer jacket. Which of the following is the b Best corrective action?

questions.learningObjective: Describe what happens when a shielded wire has both shield ends left floating
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that leaving both shield ends floating creates an ungrounded conductor that acts as a receiving/radiating antenna, which can increase EMI coupling rather than reduce it, defeating the shield's purpose.

flashcard.definition: If both ends of a cable shield are left floating (ungrounded), the shield has no return path for induced currents. It then behaves like an antenna, capable of picking up or radiating noise, and can actually worsen EMI instead of reducing it. A shield must be grounded at one end or both per the installation manual.
flashcard.analogy: An ungrounded shield is like a lightning rod with no wire to earth — it collects energy but has nowhere to send it, so the energy couples into the conductor it was meant to protect.

jeopardy.question: This is what happens when a shielded wire is installed with the shield floating at both ends.
choices: The ungrounded shield behaves as an antenna, potentially increasing EMI coupling instead of reducing it
The shield automatically grounds itself through capacitance to the airframe
The shield provides maximum EMI suppression because it is fully isolated
The shield's current-carrying capability improves significantly
jeopardy.explanation: A floating shield has no return path and acts as an antenna, degrading EMI performance instead of improving it. Capacitive coupling to structure is unreliable and non-compliant. Shields do not carry signal current, so current rating is not a factor.

multipleChoice.question: If a shielded wire is found with the shield floating at both ends, which best describes the result?
choices: The shield is providing optimal EMI protection by being fully isolated
The shield grounds itself automatically through stray capacitance to the airframe
The ungrounded shield acts as an antenna and can increase EMI coupling instead of reducing it
The shield's current-carrying capacity is improved
multipleChoice.explanation: A shield needs a low-impedance ground path to dump induced currents. Floating both ends leaves no return, and the shield becomes an antenna. Capacitive coupling to structure is not a compliant design. Shields do not conduct signal current.

practice.question: A technician finds a harness where the shield drains are cut short at both ends, with no connection to ground. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: The shield is providing maximum protection because it is fully isolated
The cable's current-carrying capacity is increased
The shield grounds itself automatically through capacitance
The ungrounded shield behaves as an antenna — it picks up or radiates noise, worsening EMI rather than reducing it
practice.explanation: An ungrounded shield cannot dissipate induced currents and becomes an EMI antenna. The proper fix is to ground the shield per the installation manual (single-point or both-ends, as specified). Capacitive coupling is not a reliable ground. Shields are not signal conductors.

preassessment.question: What happens when both ends of a wire shield are left unterminated?
choices: Maximum EMI protection
The shield grounds itself automatically
The shield acts like an antenna and can increase EMI
Improved current capacity

questions.targetCaetStem: If you discover a shielded wire with the shield floating at both ends, what occurs?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the first step in fabricating a wire harness from a wiring diagram
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that harness fabrication begins with laying out the wiring diagram and planning the harness — identifying wire runs, connector positions, and bundle lengths — before any wire is cut or terminated.

flashcard.definition: Fabrication of a wire harness starts with reviewing the wiring diagram and laying out the physical plan: identifying each wire run, connector location, and bundle length, and marking break-out points. This precedes any cutting, stripping, or crimping.
flashcard.analogy: Like a tailor who measures and marks before the first cut — laying out the harness is measuring twice so you cut once.

jeopardy.question: Working from a wiring diagram to fabricate a harness, this is typically the opening step of the process.
choices: Lay out the wiring diagram and plan the harness — identify runs, connector positions, and bundle lengths
Crimp every connector to both ends of every wire before routing
Install the harness in the aircraft and then mark the break-outs
Cut all wires to finished length and bundle them immediately
jeopardy.explanation: Layout and planning come first — identify each run, each connector, and each break-out based on the diagram. Cutting, crimping, and installing all come after the plan is verified. Jumping to cut or crimp wastes wire and produces misfit harnesses.

multipleChoice.question: A technician is about to build a wire harness from a diagram. What is usually the opening step of that process?
choices: Cut all wires to the estimated final length and bundle them together
Lay out the wiring diagram and plan the harness — identifying each wire run, connector position, and bundle length before cutting
Crimp every connector to both ends of every wire before any routing
Install the unmade harness into the aircraft and mark break-outs in place
multipleChoice.explanation: Harness fabrication begins with layout and planning from the diagram. Cutting, crimping, and installation come after the physical plan is verified. Skipping layout wastes wire, produces mismatched lengths, and usually results in rework.

practice.question: A technician is assigned to fabricate a custom harness from a manufacturer's wiring diagram. What should the technician do FIRST?
choices: Cut all wires to an estimated length and start crimping terminations
Install connectors on every wire end before any routing decisions
Place the unmade harness in the aircraft and work out the lengths while installed
Lay out the wiring diagram and plan the harness on a jig or board — identifying each run, connector location, and break-out length — before cutting any wire
practice.explanation: Professional harness fabrication begins with layout — usually on a harness board or jig — based on the wiring diagram. This establishes runs, break-outs, and lengths before wire is cut. Jumping straight to cutting, crimping, or in-aircraft fitting produces waste and rework.

preassessment.question: What is the first step in fabricating a harness from a wiring diagram?
choices: Lay out the diagram and plan the harness before cutting
Cut all wires immediately
Crimp all connectors first
Install the harness and mark break-outs in place

questions.targetCaetStem: When fabricating a wire harness from a wiring diagram, what is the FIRST step you should typically take?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the method used to confirm that a specific switch or circuit is responsible for a parasitic battery drain
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that isolating a parasitic drain to a specific circuit is done by pulling circuit breakers one at a time while monitoring battery current with an ammeter; the faulty circuit is identified when pulling its breaker causes the drain current to drop to normal.

flashcard.definition: To confirm that a faulty switch or circuit is causing a parasitic battery drain, connect an ammeter in series with the battery to monitor total draw, then pull each circuit breaker one at a time. When pulling a specific breaker causes the measured current to drop to normal, that circuit (and the switch within it) is confirmed as the source of the drain.
flashcard.analogy: Pulling breakers one at a time is like closing valves on a manifold until the leak stops — the valve that stops the leak identifies the leaking branch.

jeopardy.question: This method BEST confirms that a specific switch is the source of a parasitic battery drain.
choices: Measuring the switch's contact temperature with an infrared thermometer
Monitoring total battery current while pulling circuit breakers one at a time until the drain drops to normal
Weighing the battery before and after a 24-hour soak
Listening for audible arcing near each switch
jeopardy.explanation: Pulling breakers one at a time while watching battery current isolates the drain to a specific circuit. Temperature, weight, and audio methods cannot reliably identify a milliampere-level drain.

multipleChoice.question: Suspecting that a failed switch is behind a parasitic drain, how does the tech most reliably prove it?
choices: Measure the switch's contact temperature with an infrared thermometer and compare it to adjacent switches
Listen for audible arcing near each switch with the master on
Weigh the battery before and after a 24-hour soak to detect energy loss

Connect an ammeter in series with the battery to monitor total current, then pull each circuit breaker one at a time; when pulling a specific breaker causes the drain to drop to normal, the circuit (and its switch) is identified
multipleChoice.explanation: The CB-by-CB isolation method is the standard parasitic-drain hunt. An ammeter shows the cumulative draw; as each breaker is pulled, the drain either drops or stays. The breaker whose removal restores normal draw identifies the offending circuit. From there, the technician can confirm the specific switch. Temperature, weight, and audio checks are not sensitive enough to isolate typical parasitic drains.

practice.question: A customer complains that the aircraft battery discharges overnight. The technician suspects a faulty switch in the lighting circuit. What is the most productive way to confirm the suspicion?
choices: Install an ammeter in series with the battery, record the baseline drain with all switches off, then pull circuit breakers one at a time while watching the ammeter. The breaker whose removal causes the drain to drop to normal identifies the branch containing the faulty switch
Replace the suspected switch blindly and see if the problem disappears
Disconnect the battery and replace it
Turn on all switches and measure current to see which one spikes
practice.explanation: CB-by-CB isolation with an ammeter is the standard parasitic-draw diagnostic. It positively identifies the offending branch before parts are replaced. Blind replacement wastes parts; replacing the battery does not fix the drain; turning switches on measures load currents, not the quiescent drain that is the actual complaint.

preassessment.question: BEST confirmation for a faulty switch causing parasitic draw?
choices: Monitor battery current while pulling breakers one at a time
Infrared temperature scan
Battery weight comparison
Audible arc detection

questions.targetCaetStem: If a technician suspects a faulty switch is causing a parasitic draw, how can they BEST confirm this?

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of a bonding jumper in aircraft electrical systems
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a bonding jumper is a low-resistance conductor installed between two structures or components to maintain equipotential — allowing fault current, static charge, and lightning current to flow between the bonded parts without developing a voltage difference that could cause arcing, interference, or personnel shock.

flashcard.definition: A bonding jumper is a low-resistance conductor (typically a braided strap or dedicated wire) installed between two metal structures or components to maintain them at the same electrical potential. Its purpose is to provide a low-impedance path for fault current, static discharge, and lightning current, preventing voltage differences that could cause arcing, radio interference, or shock hazards. Typical bond resistance target is well under 10 milliohms.
flashcard.analogy: A bonding jumper is like an electrical equalizer pipe between two tanks — it prevents pressure (voltage) differences from building up between parts that should be at the same level.

jeopardy.question: On an aircraft, this is the reason a bonding jumper gets installed on the electrical system.
choices: To mechanically fasten two components together
To maintain equipotential between structures by providing a low-resistance path for fault, static, and lightning currents
To carry primary electrical power to a load
To block radio frequency signals between compartments
jeopardy.explanation: A bonding jumper ensures two structures share the same electrical potential via a low-resistance path. It is not a fastener, not a power feeder, and not an RF block.

multipleChoice.question: Why is a bonding jumper fitted on an aircraft electrical system?
choices: To provide mechanical fastening between two metal components
To supply primary electrical power from the bus to a load
To maintain equipotential between two structures or components by providing a low-resistance conductor path for fault current, static charge, and lightning current
To block radio-frequency energy from passing between two compartments
multipleChoice.explanation: A bonding jumper creates a low-resistance electrical connection (target well under 10 milliohms) between two structures so they remain at the same potential. This prevents arcing, electrical interference, and personnel shock when fault, static, or lightning currents flow. Bonding jumpers are not primary fasteners, not power feeders, and not RF shields, though good bonding does support shielding effectiveness.

practice.question: During a post-installation inspection, the technician notes a braided metal strap between the radio shelf and the surrounding aircraft structure. A trainee asks why it is there. What should the technician explain?
choices: It is a spare wire kept for future use

It is a bonding jumper that maintains the radio shelf at the same electrical potential as the surrounding structure, providing a low-resistance path so fault currents, static charge, and any lightning-induced current flow safely to structure rather than developing a voltage difference that could cause arcing or interfere with the radio
It carries primary power to the radio
It is purely decorative
practice.explanation: Bonding jumpers are everywhere in aircraft electrical systems because equipotential matters. The strap provides a low-resistance path between the shelf and structure. If a fault or static charge accumulates on the shelf, current flows through the bond rather than building up a voltage. Without the bond, the shelf could float to a different potential than the surrounding structure, causing arcing and RF interference, and creating a shock hazard during maintenance.

preassessment.question: Primary purpose of a bonding jumper?
choices: Mechanical fastening
Carry primary power
Maintain equipotential via low-resistance path for fault/static/lightning current
Block RF between compartments

questions.targetCaetStem: What is the primary purpose of a bonding jumper in aircraft electrical systems?

questions.learningObjective: Identify a plausible cause when a multimeter reads 0 V across a component in an energized circuit
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a 0 V reading across a component in an active circuit most commonly indicates the component is internally shorted (zero resistance, no voltage drop), a parallel short bypasses the component, or no current is reaching the component because the circuit is open upstream.

flashcard.term: Zero volts across a component — diagnosis
flashcard.definition: When a multimeter reads 0 V across a component in an energized circuit, the likely causes are: the component is internally shorted (R=0 means V=IR=0), a parallel conductor bypasses it, or no current is flowing through the branch because of an open-circuit fault upstream. Whether current is present elsewhere in the circuit distinguishes the 'shorted' case from the 'no current' case.
flashcard.analogy: Zero volts means either the component is a dead short (water flowing freely through a broken pipe) or there is no water flowing at all — both produce no pressure drop.

jeopardy.question: A multimeter reads 0 V across a component in a live circuit. This is a possible cause.
choices: The component is internally shorted, a parallel path bypasses it, or an upstream break has stopped current flow through that branch
The circuit is operating perfectly as designed
The meter is set to the wrong scale and should read higher
The component is dissipating maximum power
jeopardy.explanation: 0 V across a component indicates no voltage drop — either because the component has become a short, a parallel path is shunting it, or no current is flowing at all due to an upstream open.

multipleChoice.question: Across a powered component, the DMM reads 0 V. Identify a condition that could explain that.
choices: The circuit is operating as designed with the component at full rated drop
The component is dissipating its maximum rated power
The multimeter is set to the wrong scale and should display a higher value
The component has shorted internally (R ≈ 0), a parallel path is bypassing it, or an upstream break is preventing current from flowing through that branch
multipleChoice.explanation: V = IR. Zero volts across a component in an operating circuit means either resistance has collapsed to zero (the component is shorted or shunted), or current has stopped flowing through that branch (upstream open). Verifying whether current is present in the branch distinguishes these two cases. A healthy, operating component would show its expected voltage drop.

practice.question: A technician reads 0 V across a series resistor that should be dropping 5 V in an active 28 V circuit. What should the technician investigate?
choices: Conclude the resistor is fine and continue
Replace the meter because 0 V is impossible

Check whether current is actually flowing through the branch. If current is flowing, the resistor is internally shorted or has a parallel short across it. If no current is flowing, there is an upstream open circuit preventing current from reaching the resistor. Both conditions can produce a 0 V reading and each requires a different repair
Increase the bus voltage and remeasure
practice.explanation: The 0 V reading is diagnostic but ambiguous. Confirming whether current is present in the branch (via an ammeter or by measuring a voltage drop elsewhere) separates the two possibilities. A shorted resistor and an upstream open look the same at the component — confirming current flow resolves the ambiguity and guides the correct repair.

preassessment.question: The DMM shows 0 V across a powered component — explain a condition that could cause this.
choices: Component shorted, parallel short, or upstream break stopping current
Normal operation
Wrong meter scale
Maximum power dissipation

questions.targetCaetStem: If you measure 0?V across a component in an active circuit, a possible cause is:

questions.learningObjective: Identify the practices that ensure accurate measurements with a micrometer
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that accurate micrometer measurement depends on verifying zero before use (zero check on a reference surface or block), using a consistent feel via the ratchet/friction thimble to standardize contact force, keeping the anvil and spindle clean, and positioning the part squarely between the measuring faces.

flashcard.definition: For accurate micrometer readings: (1) verify the zero reading on a clean reference before measurement (zero with anvil and spindle together, or on a gauge block), (2) use the ratchet or friction thimble for consistent contact force (avoid overtightening), (3) keep the anvil and spindle faces clean and free of debris, and (4) position the part squarely between the measuring faces. These steps eliminate the most common sources of micrometer error.
flashcard.analogy: A micrometer is only as accurate as its zero and your feel — verify zero and let the ratchet do the final squeeze.

jeopardy.question: This practice is essential for accurate readings with a micrometer.
choices: Tighten the thimble as hard as possible for full engagement
Verify the zero reading first and use the ratchet or friction thimble for consistent contact force
Heat the part before measuring to expand it
Take only a single reading without repositioning
jeopardy.explanation: Zero verification and consistent feel via the ratchet are the foundation of accurate micrometer readings. Maximum thimble torque distorts the measurement; heating changes the dimension; single readings miss technique error.

multipleChoice.question: To read accurate dimensions off a micrometer, what must the technician do?
choices: Tighten the thimble as hard as possible to ensure full contact
Take a single reading without repositioning or repeating
Heat the part before measuring so the dimension stabilizes

Verify the zero reading on a clean reference before use, apply consistent contact force via the ratchet or friction thimble, keep the anvil and spindle clean, and seat the part squarely between the measuring faces
multipleChoice.explanation: Accurate micrometer work starts with verifying zero (anvil and spindle closed, or on a gauge block) and relies on a consistent 'feel' achieved through the ratchet or friction thimble — this standardizes the contact force and prevents the spindle from deforming soft parts or giving inconsistent readings. Cleanliness and square seating round out the technique. Overtightening, heating, and single measurements introduce error rather than eliminating it.

practice.question: A new technician is learning to measure a small fitting with a 0-1 inch micrometer. What habits should the technician develop from the start?
choices: Grip the thimble firmly and twist hard

Before measuring: close the anvil and spindle to confirm zero reads 0.000 inches, wipe the measuring faces clean, and use the ratchet or friction thimble to apply consistent light pressure. Position the part squarely between the faces and take two or three readings at slightly different positions to verify consistency. Learn the feel, not the force
Warm the part with your hand first
Rely on the first reading without checking
practice.explanation: Micrometer accuracy depends on zero verification, clean measuring faces, consistent contact force (provided by the ratchet or friction thimble), and square seating. Overtightening deforms both soft parts and the micrometer itself. Hand-warming expands the part and introduces error. Single readings miss positioning mistakes. Building good technique from the start avoids rework.

preassessment.question: Accurate micrometer measurement requires:
choices: Zero verification plus consistent force via ratchet
Maximum thimble torque
Heating the part
A single reading

questions.targetCaetStem: When using a micrometer, what should be done to ensure accurate measurements?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the primary purpose of a center punch in avionics fabrication
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a center punch is used to create a small conical dimple at a marked location in metal, giving a drill bit a starting point so the bit does not wander or 'walk' across the surface when drilling begins.

flashcard.definition: A center punch creates a small conical indentation in sheet metal or other hard material at a marked drill-hole location. The dimple captures the tip of the drill bit during the first moments of drilling, preventing the bit from wandering across the surface and ensuring the hole is drilled accurately at the marked location. Without a center punch, drill bits tend to walk, producing misplaced holes.
flashcard.analogy: A center punch is the starting chalk mark for a drill — it holds the bit on target until cutting takes over.

jeopardy.question: This tool creates a dimple in metal so a drill bit starts accurately without walking.
choices: A deburring tool
A tap and die set
A center punch
A machinist's scriber
jeopardy.explanation: The center punch creates the starting dimple for drilling. A deburring tool finishes hole edges, a tap cuts threads, and a scriber only marks lines without creating a dimple.

multipleChoice.question: On an avionics bench, why does the technician keep a center punch in the toolbox?
choices: To remove burrs from the edges of drilled holes
To scribe long straight layout lines on sheet metal
To cut internal threads in a drilled hole
To create a small conical dimple at a marked location so a drill bit starts accurately and does not wander across the metal surface
multipleChoice.explanation: A center punch places a conical indentation at the intended drill location. The dimple holds the drill bit's tip in place during the initial cut so the hole is drilled exactly where marked. Deburring, thread cutting, and scribing are separate operations using different tools — a deburring tool, a tap, and a scriber respectively.

practice.question: A technician has scribed hole locations on an aluminum bracket and is about to drill. Why should the technician center-punch each location before starting the drill?
choices: To harden the metal surface in preparation for drilling
To remove oxidation from the surface
To create a small dimple that captures the drill bit's tip and prevents it from walking across the surface, ensuring each hole is drilled exactly where it was marked
To improve the cosmetic appearance of the part
practice.explanation: A scribed line alone does not hold a drill bit. When the bit touches a flat metal surface it tends to wander before the cutting edge engages. A center-punched dimple captures the bit tip and locks it to the marked location, producing an accurately placed hole. Hardening, oxidation removal, and cosmetics are not why the center punch is used.

preassessment.question: Primary purpose of a center punch?
choices: Creating a drill-starting dimple in metal
Deburring
Thread cutting
Scribing layout lines

questions.targetCaetStem: What is the primary purpose of a center punch in an avionics repair shop?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the most important factor for preventing screw-head damage when using a manual screwdriver
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the most important factor in preventing screw-head damage with a manual screwdriver is selecting the correct driver tip type and size to fully engage the screw recess, applying straight axial pressure in line with the screw, so torque is distributed evenly around the engagement surface rather than concentrated where cam-out can begin.

flashcard.definition: The most important factor in preventing damage to a screw head is selecting the correct driver tip — the exact type (Phillips, slotted, Torx, Pozidriv, Robertson) and the exact size — and seating it fully in the screw recess while applying straight axial pressure along the screw axis. Correct engagement distributes torque around the full recess surface; mismatched or partially seated drivers concentrate force on a few corners and cam out, stripping the head.
flashcard.analogy: Fitting the driver to the screw is like fitting a wrench to a bolt — the right size and full engagement let you turn without rounding the corners.

jeopardy.question: This is the MOST important factor in preventing a manual screwdriver from damaging the screw head.
choices: Selecting the heaviest screwdriver handle
Matching the driver tip type and size to the screw recess and seating it fully with straight axial pressure
Applying the maximum possible torque
Coating the tip with anti-seize
jeopardy.explanation: Correct tip match and full seating distribute torque evenly and prevent cam-out. A heavy handle is not the determining factor. Maximum torque accelerates damage. Anti-seize causes slippage.

multipleChoice.question: To avoid chewing up a screw head with a hand-driven screwdriver, what is the SINGLE most important thing a technician controls?
choices: Matching the driver tip to the screw recess exactly — correct type (Phillips, slotted, Torx, etc.) and correct size — and seating it fully with straight axial pressure along the screw axis
Selecting the heaviest screwdriver handle available for better grip
Applying the maximum possible torque to overcome stuck threads
Coating the driver tip with anti-seize compound before inserting
multipleChoice.explanation: Correct tip type, correct tip size, full engagement, and straight axial pressure are the key factors. A properly matched and fully seated driver transfers torque evenly across the recess, avoiding cam-out. Mismatched or partially seated drivers concentrate force on a few corners and strip the screw head. Handle weight, maximum torque, and lubrication do not solve (and can worsen) the underlying engagement problem.

practice.question: A technician is removing a series of small Phillips screws from an avionics faceplate. Two of the screws have started showing wear at the head. What should the technician do to prevent further damage?
choices: Apply more torque and force the screws out
Lubricate the driver tip and continue

Stop, confirm the correct Phillips size is being used (typically #0, #1, or #2 for small screws), ensure the tip is fully seated, and apply firm straight-down pressure in line with the screw axis while turning. If a head is already too damaged to engage, switch to a screw extractor or damaged-screw tool before continuing
Use a longer-handled screwdriver for more leverage
practice.explanation: Continuing with a mismatched or poorly seated driver on a damaged head guarantees further damage. Correct response: verify the right tip size for the screw, seat it fully, apply straight axial pressure to keep the driver engaged, and reduce torque if the screw is stuck. If the head is past rescue, a damaged-screw extractor is the correct tool. Lubrication and longer handles do not address the engagement problem.

preassessment.question: MOST important factor to prevent screw-head damage?
choices: Correct tip type/size with full engagement and straight pressure
Heavy handle
Maximum torque
Anti-seize on the tip

questions.targetCaetStem: When using a manual screwdriver, what is the MOST important factor to consider to prevent damage to the screw head?

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary use of a milliohmmeter in avionics maintenance
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a milliohmmeter is primarily used to measure very low resistances in the milliohm range — typically on bonding straps, ground paths, relay contacts, and connector contacts — where a standard DMM's ohms range cannot resolve values of a few milliohms and its lead resistance would dominate the reading.

flashcard.definition: A milliohmmeter measures very low resistances in the milliohm range (thousandths of an ohm). It is used for bonding-strap verification, ground-path checks, relay and connector contact resistance, and conductor integrity. A four-wire Kelvin connection eliminates the lead and contact resistance that would otherwise dominate a standard DMM reading, giving a true measurement of the resistance under test.
flashcard.analogy: A milliohmmeter is the precision tool for measuring 'near-zero' resistance — where a standard DMM just reads 'close to zero' without the resolution to tell a good bond from a marginal one.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary use of a milliohmmeter.
choices: Applying a high DC test voltage to insulation
Measuring very low resistances in the milliohm range, such as bonding straps and relay contacts, using a four-wire Kelvin connection
Displaying voltage waveforms over time
Measuring AC signal frequency
jeopardy.explanation: A milliohmmeter reads very low resistances with four-wire Kelvin technique. Insulation resistance requires a megger. Waveform display requires an oscilloscope. Frequency requires a counter.

multipleChoice.question: On the shop bench, a milliohmmeter is the instrument of choice for measuring:
choices: Apply a high DC test voltage to measure insulation resistance
Display voltage waveforms as a function of time

Measure very low resistances in the milliohm range — bonding straps, ground paths, relay contacts, and connector contacts — using a four-wire Kelvin connection that eliminates lead and contact resistance
Measure the frequency of an AC signal
multipleChoice.explanation: A milliohmmeter measures resistances in the thousandths-of-an-ohm range. Its four-wire Kelvin technique forces a known current through the part under test and measures voltage with separate leads, so lead and contact resistance do not contaminate the reading. Typical uses are bonding verification (where the target is well under 10 milliohms), relay contacts, and high-current connectors. Insulation resistance requires a megger; waveforms require an oscilloscope; frequency requires a counter.

practice.question: A technician needs to verify that a newly installed bonding strap reads less than 5 milliohms per the installation procedure. Which instrument is appropriate, and why?
choices: A milliohmmeter, because the target value is in milliohms. A standard DMM's lead and contact resistance alone would be several hundred milliohms, completely masking the real bond resistance. The milliohmmeter uses a four-wire Kelvin connection that eliminates lead resistance from the reading
A standard DMM on the ohms range, because it can read resistance
A megohmmeter at 500 V
An oscilloscope
practice.explanation: The target measurement is in milliohms, which is beyond the usable resolution of a standard DMM. DMM lead resistance alone (typically 200-500 milliohms) is orders of magnitude larger than the bond being measured and would completely dominate the reading. A milliohmmeter's four-wire Kelvin technique separates the current-injection leads from the voltage-sense leads, eliminating lead resistance from the measurement. Meggers measure insulation, not bonding. Oscilloscopes show waveforms.

preassessment.question: On the shop bench, a milliohmmeter measures:
choices: Insulation resistance
Signal frequency
Voltage waveforms
Very low resistance (bonding, contacts) with Kelvin connection

questions.targetCaetStem: A milliohmmeter is primarily used to:

questions.learningObjective: Identify the category of hazard covered by a Class C fire rating
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Class C on the U.S. fire classification system covers fires involving energized electrical equipment (wiring, motors, avionics, tools) and that extinguishers used on Class C fires must be non-conductive agents such as CO2 or dry chemical — never water or water-based agents.

flashcard.term: Class C fire — what it covers
flashcard.definition: Under the U.S. fire classification system (A, B, C, D, K), Class C covers fires involving energized electrical equipment: wiring, motors, avionics, test benches, and powered tools. Because the equipment is live, the extinguishing agent must be non-conductive. CO2 and dry-chemical extinguishers are approved; water and water-based foams are prohibited because they conduct current and create a shock hazard for the operator.
flashcard.analogy: Think of Class C as the 'Current' fire — the circuit is still live, so only a non-conductive agent can safely put it out.

jeopardy.question: A Class C fire rating covers fires that involve this type of hazard.
choices: Ordinary combustibles such as wood, paper, and rags
Flammable liquids such as solvents, gasoline, and hydraulic fluid
Energized electrical equipment such as wiring, motors, and avionics
Combustible metals such as magnesium and sodium
jeopardy.explanation: Class A covers ordinary combustibles; Class B covers flammable liquids; Class C covers energized electrical equipment; Class D covers combustible metals; Class K covers cooking oils. The technician must match the extinguisher to the class of fire.

multipleChoice.question: A Class C fire rating covers fires that involve which type of hazard?
choices: Ordinary combustibles such as wood, paper, and shop rags
Combustible metals such as magnesium, sodium, and lithium
Flammable liquids such as solvents, gasoline, and hydraulic fluid
Energized electrical equipment — wiring, motors, avionics, and powered shop tools — which requires a non-conductive extinguishing agent such as CO2 or dry chemical
multipleChoice.explanation: Class C is the electrical-equipment class. Because the equipment is still live, only non-conductive agents (CO2, dry chemical) may be used. Class A covers ordinary combustibles, Class B covers flammable liquids, Class D covers combustible metals, and Class K covers cooking oils. Selecting the wrong extinguisher class can electrocute the operator or spread the fire.

practice.question: A technician sees the letter C on the faceplate of a fire extinguisher mounted next to an avionics test bench. What category of fire is that extinguisher rated to fight?
choices: Fires in paper, wood, or shop rags

Fires involving energized electrical equipment — the C rating tells the technician the extinguisher is safe to use on live wiring, motors, avionics, and powered bench equipment. The agent is non-conductive (CO2 or dry chemical), so the operator is not exposed to a shock hazard while discharging it
Fires in gasoline, solvent, or hydraulic fluid spills
Fires in magnesium or sodium metal chips
practice.explanation: The C rating means the extinguisher is approved for energized electrical equipment. That is exactly why it is mounted by the test bench — the most likely fire in that area is a short or overheat on a live circuit. A Class A would be for the rag bin, a Class B for a solvent cabinet, and a Class D for a metals-machining area. Using a water extinguisher on a Class C fire risks electrocution.

preassessment.question: Class C fires involve what?
choices: Wood and paper
Flammable liquids
Energized electrical equipment
Combustible metals

questions.targetCaetStem: Class C fires are commonly associated with:

questions.learningObjective: Identify the first step to ensure proper grounding when installing a new avionics system
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that before connecting any energized circuit or test equipment to a newly installed avionics system, the first step is to establish an equipotential ground reference — verifying that the aircraft structure, the unit's ground, the bonding straps, and the test equipment ground are all tied to a single low-impedance reference — per the installation manual and AC 43.13.

flashcard.term: Proper grounding — first step on a new install
flashcard.definition: Before energizing a new avionics system, the first step in establishing proper grounding is to create an equipotential ground reference: the aircraft structure, the unit's chassis ground, the bonding jumpers, and any test-equipment ground must all be tied to a single low-impedance reference. This prevents ground loops, protects the technician from differential-voltage shock, and ensures correct operation of shielded cables and signal references.
flashcard.analogy: Think of grounding like laying a foundation — every component has to stand on the same reference level before you turn on the power, or the whole system will be uneven and unsafe.

jeopardy.question: A technician begins a new-panel install and the unit must be grounded. This is the initial action to establish correct grounding.
choices: Apply aircraft power and measure the voltage at the case
Check that the pilot's seat rails are bonded to the airframe
Create an equipotential reference by bonding the chassis, the airframe, and any bench test gear to one low-impedance point per the install manual
Tie the antenna coax shield to the headset jack
jeopardy.explanation: Equipotential bonding before power-up is the initial step. Measuring under power skips the prerequisite. Seat and headset bonding are unrelated to the new system's ground reference.

multipleChoice.question: A technician is beginning an installation that calls for a chassis ground to airframe structure. Which action must be completed before energizing the unit?
choices: Create an equipotential reference by bonding the chassis, the bonding jumpers, the airframe structure, and any bench test equipment to one low-impedance ground point per the install manual and AC 43.13
Apply aircraft power and then check case voltage to see if the ground appears acceptable
Confirm the pilot's seat rails are bonded to the airframe before doing anything else
Tie the antenna coax shield to the headset jack ground
multipleChoice.explanation: Bonding must be established before energizing the unit. The standard procedure is to bond the new unit's chassis, the aircraft structure, any bonding straps, and test equipment to a single low-impedance reference, following the installation manual and AC 43.13-1B guidance. This prevents differential voltages, eliminates ground loops, and protects the technician. Applying power first, or bonding unrelated items, is unsafe and non-compliant.

practice.question: A shop is about to bench-install a new GPS navigator whose installation manual calls for a chassis bond to airframe structure. The unit is out of the box and the mounting tray is in place. Which action must be completed before any power-on testing?
choices: Route power to the unit and look at the display to confirm it is working

Create an equipotential reference first: follow the installation manual's bonding diagram to tie the chassis, the bonding jumpers, the mounting tray, nearby airframe structure, and any bench test equipment to one low-impedance point. Verify bond resistance with a milliohm meter where the manual specifies. Only after bonding is verified does the technician route power and begin functional tests
Confirm that the pilot's seat rails are bonded to the airframe before anything else
Clip the antenna coax shield to the nearest headset jack as a ground
practice.explanation: The correct first action is equipotential bonding in accordance with the installation manual. Bond resistance is typically required to be in the low-milliohm range. Skipping this step and applying power risks damaging the unit, creating a shock hazard to the technician, and producing intermittent operation once the aircraft returns to service. AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 covers bonding and grounding practices.

preassessment.question: Before energizing a newly installed avionics unit, what must be done?
choices: Apply power first
Establish equipotential bond per the install manual
Bond the pilot seat
Clip coax shield to headset jack

questions.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are tasked with installing a new avionics system that requires grounding. What is the first step to ensure proper grounding?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the recommended action when a hydraulic fluid spill occurs near an avionics bench
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the recommended response to a hydraulic fluid spill near an avionics bench is to stop work, alert others, contain the spill with approved absorbents (never shop rags alone), ventilate the area, avoid ignition sources, clean up per the SDS, and dispose of the contaminated absorbent as hazardous waste.

flashcard.definition: A hydraulic fluid spill near an avionics bench is both a slip hazard and a fire hazard (most hydraulic fluids are combustible). The correct response: stop work and alert others, block ignition sources, contain the spill with approved absorbent (pads, pillows, granular absorbent — not shop rags alone), ventilate the area, follow the SDS for cleanup, and dispose of contaminated absorbent as hazardous waste in a labeled container.
flashcard.analogy: A fluid spill is like a leak in a kitchen — stop, warn, contain, clean, and dispose properly rather than walking away or covering it with a newspaper.

jeopardy.question: A puddle of hydraulic fluid has leaked onto the floor next to an avionics workstation. This is the correct response.
choices: Continue working and clean up the puddle at the end of the shift
Push the fluid into the closest floor drain with a broom
Pour thinner over the puddle to dilute it

Halt work and warn coworkers, remove nearby ignition sources, absorb the pool with an approved absorbent pad or granule, clean per the SDS, and place the contaminated absorbent into a labeled hazmat container
jeopardy.explanation: Halt, warn, contain, clean per SDS, and dispose as hazmat. Continuing to work creates slip and fire risk. Solvent makes a worse mixture. Drain disposal violates environmental regulations.

multipleChoice.question: A puddle of hydraulic fluid has leaked onto the floor next to an avionics workstation. What is the correct response?
choices: Continue working and clean up the puddle at the end of the shift when there is more time
Push the fluid into the closest floor drain with a broom
Pour thinner over the puddle to dilute the fluid and then walk away

Halt work and warn coworkers out of the area, remove ignition sources, absorb the pool with an approved absorbent pad or granule, ventilate, clean up per the SDS, and place the contaminated absorbent into a labeled hazmat container for certified disposal
multipleChoice.explanation: Hydraulic fluid is both a slip hazard and a combustible liquid. The required response is containment with approved absorbent, control of ignition sources, SDS-directed cleanup, and disposal of contaminated absorbent as hazardous waste. Ignoring the puddle invites injury. Solvents can increase the fire hazard. Drain disposal violates the Clean Water Act.

practice.question: During a routine avionics installation, a pressurized hydraulic line leaks onto the floor next to the avionics bench, creating a pool about three feet across. Bystanders are walking through the shop. What is the correct immediate response?
choices: Finish the current task first, then mop up the fluid at the end of the shift
Pour mineral spirits on the spill to thin it out and then move on

Stop work and warn personnel out of the area; eliminate nearby ignition sources (unplug heaters, post 'no smoking'); place an absorbent pad or boom around the perimeter and lay absorbent granules over the pool; open ventilation; follow the fluid's SDS for cleanup; and place the contaminated absorbent into a properly labeled hazmat drum for certified disposal
Push the pooled fluid toward the floor drain with a shop broom
practice.explanation: Hydraulic fluids are typically combustible and always a slip risk. The correct sequence is stop-warn-contain-clean-dispose. Approved absorbents are used; contaminated absorbent is hazmat (not regular trash). Floor-drain disposal violates federal and local environmental rules and can contaminate waterways. Thinning the spill with a solvent only makes cleanup harder and increases fire risk.

preassessment.question: Hydraulic fluid spilled near the bench — recommended action?
choices: Stop, alert, contain with approved absorbent, clean per SDS, hazmat disposal
Finish work, clean at end of shift
Pour solvent on it
Sweep into floor drain

questions.targetCaetStem: There is a hydraulic fluid spill near the avionics bench Recommended action?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the correct method for storing flammable liquids in an avionics shop
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that flammable liquids in an avionics shop must be stored in a UL-listed (or FM-approved) flammable-liquid storage cabinet that is grounded, properly vented per local code, kept closed when not in use, labeled 'Flammable — Keep Fire Away,' and located away from heat, ignition sources, and exits.

flashcard.definition: Flammable liquids must be stored in a UL-listed (or FM-approved) flammable-liquid storage cabinet. The cabinet must be grounded, vented per local code, kept closed when not in use, labeled 'Flammable — Keep Fire Away,' and located away from heat sources, ignition sources, and exits. Free-standing cans on benches, storage inside electrical enclosures, and refrigerator-style household cabinets are all non-compliant.
flashcard.analogy: A flammable-liquid cabinet is like a small safe for the fire-hazard chemicals — listed, labeled, grounded, and closed until you need something inside.

jeopardy.question: This is the compliant method for keeping flammable chemicals in an avionics maintenance facility.
choices: Loose on an open bench next to the soldering station
In cardboard cartons stacked near the shop exit
Inside a household refrigerator converted to chemical storage
In a UL-listed (or FM-approved) flammable-liquid cabinet that is grounded, properly vented, kept closed when not in use, and labeled 'Flammable — Keep Fire Away'
jeopardy.explanation: UL-listed / FM-approved cabinets are the standard. Open benches create ignition risk. Household refrigerators are an ignition source (the compressor arcs). Cardboard near the exit blocks egress and spreads fire.

multipleChoice.question: What is the compliant method for keeping flammable chemicals in an avionics maintenance facility?
choices: In a UL-listed (or FM-approved) flammable-liquid cabinet that is grounded, vented per local code, kept closed when not in use, labeled 'Flammable — Keep Fire Away,' and located away from heat and ignition sources
Loose on an open bench next to the soldering station for easy access
Inside a household refrigerator converted to cold chemical storage
In cardboard shipping cartons stacked near the shop exit
multipleChoice.explanation: The correct method is a UL-listed (or FM-approved) flammable-liquid cabinet: grounded, vented, closed when not in use, and labeled. Open-bench storage exposes the liquid to ignition sources and spills. Converted household refrigerators are dangerous because internal thermostats and compressor relays arc. Cardboard storage near an exit creates fire load and blocks egress.

practice.question: A shop manager is setting up a flammable-liquid storage area for IPA, contact cleaner, and a small quantity of naphtha used on the avionics bench. Which storage method is compliant?
choices: Leave the bottles open on a shelf above the soldering station so technicians can reach them quickly

Install a UL-listed (or FM-approved) flammable-liquid storage cabinet: grounded via a bonding strap, vented per local fire code (or sealed with vent caps if code permits), self-closing doors kept latched when not in use, outside labeling 'Flammable — Keep Fire Away,' and placement at least several feet from soldering stations, heaters, and exits. Keep inventory inside the cabinet limited to daily-use quantities
Convert a household refrigerator to chemical storage because it seals the fumes inside
Stack the shipping cartons near the main exit so nothing blocks the benches
practice.explanation: The NFPA/OSHA approach is a listed flammable-liquid cabinet with grounding, venting, self-closing latched doors, and outside labeling. Distance from ignition sources and exits is also required. Open-shelf storage above a soldering station is dangerous — a tipped bottle plus a hot iron is an ignition event. Household refrigerators arc internally and have caused fatal lab fires. Storage near an exit blocks egress during a fire.

preassessment.question: What is the correct storage practice for flammable liquids in an avionics shop?
choices: Open bench near soldering
Cardboard cartons near exit
Household refrigerator
UL-listed flammable-liquid cabinet, grounded, vented, closed, labeled

questions.targetCaetStem: How should flammable liquids be stored in an avionics shop?

questions.learningObjective: Identify a safe practice when working on the ground around a moving or running rotorcraft
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that safe ground practices around a moving or running rotorcraft include approaching only in the pilot's view (roughly the 3 and 9 o'clock positions), staying crouched while inside the rotor disc area, waiting for the pilot's signal before approaching, and never walking under the tail rotor or the rising side of a sloping rotor disc.

flashcard.term: Safe practice around a running rotorcraft
flashcard.definition: When working on the ground around a moving or running rotorcraft, approach only within the pilot's direct line of sight (roughly 3 and 9 o'clock in most helicopters), wait for the pilot's signal, crouch as you pass inside the rotor disc, never walk aft of the cabin toward the tail rotor, and never approach on the uphill side of a sloping rotor disc. The tail rotor is usually invisible at speed and is lethal on contact.
flashcard.analogy: Treat the rotor disc like a meat slicer on a slope — visible in front, invisible on the sides, and always stay low inside its reach.

jeopardy.question: A helicopter is running on the ramp and a technician must approach it. This describes the correct ground-safety practice.
choices: Walk aft of the cabin and around the tail rotor to save time
Run under the rotor disc to minimize exposure time
Approach on the uphill side of a helicopter parked on sloping ground
Enter the pilot's field of view at roughly 3 or 9 o'clock, wait for the pilot's signal, crouch while inside the rotor disc, and stay clear of the tail rotor region
jeopardy.explanation: Pilot line-of-sight approach, pilot's signal, crouched movement, and tail-rotor avoidance are the established rules. Walking near the tail rotor or on the uphill side of a sloping disc puts the technician in the blade strike zone.

multipleChoice.question: A helicopter is running on the ramp and a technician must approach it. Which option describes the correct ground-safety practice?
choices: Entering the pilot's field of view at roughly 3 or 9 o'clock, waiting for the pilot's signal, crouching while inside the rotor disc, and staying well clear of the tail rotor and the uphill side of a sloping rotor disc
Walking aft of the cabin and around the tail rotor to save a few steps
Approaching on the uphill side of a helicopter parked on sloping terrain so the disc clears the technician's head
Running under the rotor disc to keep exposure short
multipleChoice.explanation: Safe helicopter ground practice: stay in the pilot's line of sight at 3 and 9 o'clock, wait for a clear signal, crouch under the disc (blades flex downward with gusts), keep well clear of the tail rotor (which is often invisible and lethal), and never approach on the uphill side of a sloping disc — the blade tip can dip to head level. Walking aft or running under the disc ignores these rules and has caused fatal strikes.

practice.question: A technician is sent out to a customer's helicopter that has just landed with its rotor still turning. The pilot is still in the cockpit and needs to speak with the technician. How should the technician approach?
choices: Walk briskly to the tail of the helicopter to stay out of the rotor disc
Approach from the uphill side because the disc is higher overhead

Move into the pilot's view on the 3 or 9 o'clock side, stop outside the disc, establish eye contact, and wait for the pilot's thumbs-up signal. Then approach in a crouched posture, keep the main rotor blades above head level, never cross to the tail-rotor side of the cabin, and if the helicopter is on sloping ground, approach from the downhill side only. If anything feels unsafe, stop and wait
Duck under the rotor and run straight to the cockpit door to save time
practice.explanation: The standard procedure is pilot line-of-sight approach, pilot signal, crouched movement, and total avoidance of the tail rotor and the uphill side of a sloping disc. The tail rotor is essentially invisible at full RPM and kills anyone who contacts it. On sloping ground the main rotor tip on the uphill side can drop to head height. Running under the disc is dangerous because gusts or cyclic inputs can flex the blades downward.

preassessment.question: Safe practice around a moving rotorcraft?
choices: Walk aft past the tail rotor
Approach on the uphill side
Approach in pilot's view at 3/9, wait for signal, crouch, avoid tail rotor
Run under the disc

questions.targetCaetStem: Which of the following best describes a safe practice around moving rotorcraft?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the biggest technician-safety concern when working on aircraft systems that transmit radiofrequency energy
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the biggest safety concern when working on aircraft systems that transmit RF energy (weather radar, transponders, radio altimeters, HF comms) is personnel exposure to the transmitted beam — which can cause thermal injury to the eyes and deep tissue, and requires observing published keep-out zones, de-energizing transmitters for close work, and capping or covering antennas.

flashcard.term: RF transmit systems — biggest safety concern
flashcard.definition: When working on aircraft systems that transmit RF energy (weather radar, transponders, radio altimeters, HF comms), the biggest concern is personnel exposure to the transmitted beam. RF energy at close range can cause thermal burns to deep tissue and permanent injury to the eyes. Required practices: observe published keep-out zones, de-energize transmitters for close work (pull breakers and tag), cap or cover transmitting antennas, and follow the maintenance manual's RF-safety notes.
flashcard.analogy: A radar transmitter at close range is like a microwave oven with the door open — you cannot see the beam, but it cooks tissue just the same.

jeopardy.question: A technician services a transmitter such as weather radar or a transponder. This is a leading safety concern.
choices: The paint finish on the radome fading from sunlight
Interference with nearby consumer Bluetooth headsets in the shop
The possibility that the avionics bays will overheat during maintenance
Bodily exposure to the transmitted beam, which can cause thermal injury to the eyes and deep tissue and requires observing keep-out zones and de-energizing transmitters
jeopardy.explanation: Personnel RF exposure is the primary shop-safety concern. Paint fading, bay overheating, and consumer-electronics interference are not technician-injury hazards.

multipleChoice.question: A technician is servicing a transmitter such as weather radar or a transponder. Which item is a leading safety concern?
choices: Bodily exposure to the transmitted beam — especially to the eyes and deep tissue — which requires observing published keep-out zones, de-energizing transmitters for close work, and capping or covering transmitting antennas
The paint finish on the radome fading from prolonged sunlight exposure
The possibility that the avionics bay will overheat during bench work
Interference with nearby consumer Bluetooth headsets inside the hangar
multipleChoice.explanation: Personnel exposure to the transmitted beam is the primary RF-safety concern. The eyes and testes are especially vulnerable to thermal injury from concentrated beams at close range. Keep-out zones, transmitter de-energization, and antenna covers are standard mitigations. Paint fade, bay overheat, and Bluetooth interference are not technician-injury hazards.

practice.question: A technician is about to remove and reinstall the weather radar flat-plate antenna in the nose of a turboprop. The aircraft is in the hangar. What is the single biggest RF-safety concern the technician must address before starting work?
choices: Whether the radome paint will match after reassembly

Personnel exposure to the transmitted RF beam if the radar is inadvertently energized. Before any close-in work, pull and tag the radar circuit breaker, place a warning placard in the cockpit, cap or cover the antenna face, and confirm with the maintenance manual's keep-out zones. Never work in front of an energized radar antenna
Whether the avionics bay temperature will rise during the job
Whether the radar might interfere with a Bluetooth headset in the shop
practice.explanation: Personnel RF exposure is the primary hazard. High-power transmitters such as weather radar can cause thermal burns to deep tissue and permanent eye injury at close range. Mandatory protections: pull the breaker, tag and placard, cap the antenna, and consult the manual's keep-out zones. Paint, bay temperature, and consumer-electronics interference are not life-safety issues.

preassessment.question: Biggest concern on RF-emitting systems?
choices: Radome paint
Bay overheat
Personnel exposure to the transmitted RF beam
Bluetooth interference

questions.targetCaetStem: When working on aircraft systems that emit radiofrequency (RF) signals, one of the biggest concerns is:

questions.learningObjective: Identify where to find specific safety information about a chemical solvent used for avionics cleaning
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) — provided by the chemical manufacturer and kept readily accessible in the shop per OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard — is the authoritative source for chemical-specific information on hazards, PPE, first aid, handling, storage, spill response, and disposal.

flashcard.term: Where to find safety info on a shop chemical
